The Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is the single most important element in project planning. It is a product-oriented family tree subdivision of the hardware, services, and data required to produce the end product.

The WBS organizes work into elements that are manageable, independent, integratable, and measurable.

WBS Levels (Example Six-Level Structure):

Level 1: Total Program

Level 2: Project

Level 3: Task (Summary levels for reporting)

Level 4: Subtask

Level 5: Work Package (The level at which the project is managed)

Level 6: Level of Effort.


The WBS provides the common framework for cost/budget setting, schedule establishment, risk analysis, and establishing the responsibility matrix.

WBS Decomposition Problems: Breaking work down too far can lead to excessive control/reporting costs that exceed the benefits.

Work Package Characteristics:

Definable start and end dates.

Budgeted in terms of dollars, man-hours, and performance measurement.

Assignable to a single manager or cost account.

Short duration (usually less than three months).



7.12.6 Project Baselines
A baseline provides a reference point for measuring performance. The most critical reference is the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB), which serves as the metric benchmark against which time, cost, and scope performance are measured.
Steps to Creating a PMB:

Review the business case and constraints (understanding the business boundary).

Establish a requirements baseline from the customer’s requirements/CSOW (technical boundary).

Convert the requirements baseline into a WBS (WBS + WBS Dictionary = Scope Baseline).

Arrange work packages into a logical network of activities (Schedule Baseline).

Price out the time-phased schedule (Cost Baseline, which includes distributed and undistributed budgets, but excludes the management reserve).

Finalize the PMB (Summation of Scope, Schedule, and Cost Baselines).

Prepare a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) linking requirements to the WBS and PMB.


Chapter 11 Recap

A Business Case provides justification, strategic objectives, metrics, and mandatory Exit Strategies.

All assumptions, especially Critical Assumptions, must be tracked, as changes invalidate expected benefits.

Objectives must be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic/Relevant, Time-bound).

The WBS is the foundational planning tool, decomposing the project into manageable Work Packages.

Project selection often uses Rating Models (Scaling, Checklist, Scoring) to compare alternatives against strategic criteria.

The Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) integrates the scope, schedule, and cost baselines, providing the benchmark for performance tracking.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.13 Network Scheduling Techniques

7.13.1 Common Scheduling Techniques
Scheduling techniques provide control and visualization for time-phased resource utilization. Common techniques include:

Gantt or Bar Charts: Simple visualizations but poor for showing interdependencies.

Milestone Charts: Displaying key objectives, often based on WBS levels.

Networks (PERT/CPM/PDM): Show interdependencies and the total program flow, helping eliminate crisis management.



7.13.2 Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)
PERT is a management planning and control tool that uses three time estimates (optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic) to derive an expected time for each activity.

PERT is Probabilistic: It is based on a beta distribution for activity time, allowing calculation of the project completion risk.

CPM is Deterministic: It uses a single time estimate representing the normal time.



7.13.3 Slack Time and the Critical Path
The Critical Path is the longest path through the network, determining the earliest completion time of the project.

Slack Time (or Float): The amount of time an event or activity can be delayed without affecting the end date of the project or the critical path.

Events on the critical path have zero slack (TL - TE = 0, where TL is the latest allowable time and TE is the earliest expected time).


Dependencies are relationships between project activities, often derived from the WBS, and can be mandatory (hard logic) or discretionary (soft logic).



7.13.4 Estimating Total Project Time
For activities involving uncertainty (common in R&D or new projects), the PM gathers three time estimates:

$a = $ Optimistic time (best case)

$m = $ Most likely time

$b = $ Pessimistic time (worst case)

Expected Time (\(t\_e\)) Calculation: \(t_e = (a + 4m + b) / 6\).
The standard deviation (\(\sigma\)) measures the risk of the estimates. The probability of completion within a certain time frame can be calculated using the total path standard deviation (\(\sigma_{total}\)), assuming a normal distribution.


7.13.5 12.8 Total PERT/CPM Planning
Six-Step PERT/CPM Scheduling Process:

List the required activities/events.

Determine the sequence of activities (precedence).

Construct the network (diagram).

Estimate activity times (\(t\_e\)) and calculate the earliest start/finish and latest start/finish times.

Determine the Critical Path (the first iteration) and check if it satisfies calendar requirements.

Place calendar dates on each event. This crucial step converts the plan from an unlimited resource environment (the proposal) to a limited resource environment.



7.13.6 12.14 Schedule Compression Techniques
When the critical path is too long, schedule compression is used.








Technique
Myth
Reality




Use of Overtime
Work will progress at the same rate.
Progress rate is lower, mistakes increase, and burnout may occur.


Adding More Resources (Crashing)
Always shortens the schedule.
Adding more people may make it worse (Brooks’ Law), especially for complex tasks.


Fast-Tracking (Concurrent Scheduling)
Saves time with no risk.
Saves little time but increases risks (e.g., rework).




Chapter 12 Recap

Network scheduling techniques like PERT (probabilistic, three estimates) and CPM (deterministic, single estimate) are superior to bar charts for showing activity interdependencies.

The Critical Path is the longest path through the network, determining project duration.

Slack Time is the allowable delay for non-critical activities.

The \(\sigma\) (standard deviation) calculated from the three-point estimates serves as a measure of the risk in the time estimate.

The six steps of PERT/CPM planning culminate in placing calendar dates on the schedule, reflecting resource constraints.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.14 Pricing and Estimating

7.14.1 Global Pricing Strategies
Pricing strategies differ based on the long-term potential of the project acquisition.








Strategy Element
Type I Acquisition (One-of-a-Kind)
Type II Acquisition (Must-Win/Follow-On Potential)




Pricing Basis
Determined by actual cost.
Determined by market forces.


Margin
Must include gross profit margin.
Margin could be negative if winning is paramount for market penetration.





7.14.2 Types of Estimates
Estimates are critical for profitability. The accuracy improves as the project scope becomes better defined [491f].










Estimating Method
Generic Type
WBS Relationship
Accuracy
Time to Prepare




Parametric
Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM)
Top Down
\(\pm\) 25% - 75%
Days


Analogy
Budget
Top Down
\(\pm\) 10% - 25%
Weeks


Engineering/Definitive
Grass Roots
Bottom Up
\(\pm\) 5% - 10%
Months





7.14.3 Pricing Out the Work: 13 Logical Steps
The pricing process is iterative. Key steps include:

Provide a complete definition of the work requirements.

Establish a logic network with checkpoints.

Develop the WBS.

Price out the WBS.

Review WBS costs with functional managers.

Decide on the basic course of action.

Establish reasonable costs for each WBS element.

Review the base case costs with upper-level management.

Negotiate with functional managers for qualified personnel.

Develop the Linear Responsibility Chart.

Develop the final detailed schedules.

Establish pricing cost summary reports.

Document the result in a project plan.



7.14.4 Estimating High-Risk Projects
For high-risk projects (e.g., R&D where the future scope depends on early results), the “Rolling Wave” or “Moving Window” approach is used.

This technique estimates the near-term effort (e.g., next six months) in high detail (Level 5 WBS), while estimating the far-term effort at a high level (Level 2 WBS), incurring high risk for the later phases [455, 507f]. As the project progresses, the window moves, requiring refinement of the estimates for the next near-term phase.



7.14.5 The 10 Percent Solution
The 10 percent solution occurs when an executive committee arbitrarily reduces a project budget by a small percentage (e.g., 10%) without reducing scope. If the original estimate was honest, this reduction must be accompanied by a trade-off in time or performance. Often, 90% of the budget generates the first 10% of the desired output, and the remaining 10% generates 90% of the target requirements; thus, a small budget cut can lead to massive performance loss.

Viable Alternatives to the 10% Solution: (1) Use the solution only after an “impact study” detailing effects on time/cost/performance, or (2) Cancel or descope selected projects entirely.



7.14.6 Financial Project Evaluation Techniques
Capital budgeting is the decision-making process for evaluating major fixed asset purchases.

Payback Period: The time required to recover the initial investment from cash inflows. Least precise as it does not adjust for the time value of money.

Net Present Value (NPV): Equates discounted cash flows (\(FV_t\)) against the initial investment (\(II\)). If NPV \(> 0\), the project is financially desirable.

Internal Rate of Return (IRR): The discount rate where the NPV equals zero. If IRR is greater than the firm’s cost of capital, the project is acceptable.

Capital Rationing: Selecting the best group of projects to achieve the highest overall NPV without exceeding the total available budget.


Chapter 13 Recap

Pricing strategies depend on whether the acquisition is Type I (profit-driven) or Type II (market penetration/must-win).

Estimates range in accuracy from the Parametric ROM (\(\pm\) 25%-75%) to the highly accurate Definitive Bottom-Up (\(\pm\) 5%-10%).

The Rolling Wave approach is critical for high-risk, long-term projects to maintain high estimation accuracy for the near term while forecasting the far term at a high WBS level.

The arbitrary application of the 10 Percent Solution can drastically reduce performance and value.

Financial viability is evaluated using sophisticated capital budgeting methods like NPV and IRR.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.15 Cost Control

7.15.1 Management Cost and Control System (MCCS)
The MCCS is not just a tool for monitoring costs but an integrated system that monitors schedule and performance as well. An effective MCCS must:

Plan and schedule work.

Identify measurement indicators.

Establish direct labor and overhead budgets.

Identify the management reserve.

The MCCS uses the WBS as the framework to compare actual costs, time, and performance against the budget.


7.15.2 Variance and Earned Value
The Earned Value Measurement System (EVMS) integrates scope, schedule, and cost performance. Four primary elements are used:

Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS): The dollar value of the work planned to be completed as of a measurement time (Planned Value, PV).

Budgeted Cost of Work Performed (BCWP): The dollar value of the work actually accomplished (Earned Value, EV).

Actual Cost of Work Performed (ACWP): The actual cost incurred for the work accomplished (Actual Cost, AC).

Budget at Completion (BAC): The total planned budget for the project or work package.


7.15.2.1 Variance Calculation
Cost Variance (CV): \(CV = BCWP - ACWP\).

CV \(> 0\) (Positive): Under budget/favorable.

CV \(<0\) (Negative): Over budget/unfavorable.

Schedule Variance (SV): \(SV = BCWP - BCWS\).

SV \(> 0\) (Positive): Ahead of schedule/favorable.

SV \(< 0\) (Negative): Behind schedule/unfavorable.



7.15.2.2 Performance Indices (Trend Indicators)

Cost Performance Index (CPI): \(CPI = BCWP / ACWP\).

\(CPI \> 1.0\): Project is favorable (under budget).

\(CPI < 1.0\): Project is unfavorable (over budget).


Schedule Performance Index (SPI): \(SPI = BCWP / BCWS\).

\(SPI \> 1.0\): Project is favorable (ahead of schedule).

\(SPI < 1.0\): Project is unfavorable (behind schedule).





7.15.3 Budget Breakdown and Reserve
The Cost Baseline represents the time-phased authorized budget. It includes the distributed and undistributed budgets.

Management Reserves: Funds included in the total project budget to cover unforeseen events within the defined project scope (known unknowns), but they are excluded from the cost baseline.

Contingency Funds: Typically come from external sources and are used for unlikely major events or changes in scope (unknown unknowns).



7.15.4 The Cost Overrun Dilemma
When overruns occur, the simplest solution is reducing scope. Project managers often look first to easy-to-cut items which were poorly understood and often underestimated during planning, such as:

Project management supervision.

Line management supervision.

Process controls, quality assurance, and testing.

If further cuts are needed, they move to hard-to-cut items like direct labor, materials, and equipment.

Chapter 14 Recap

The MCCS is an integrated control tool tracking cost, time, and performance.

The Earned Value Management System (EVMS) uses four metrics (BCWS, BCWP, ACWP, BAC) to measure project status and forecast future outcomes.

Cost Variance (CV) and Schedule Variance (SV) measure deviation from the plan.

The CPI and SPI provide trend analysis, indicating efficiency (CPI) and schedule pace (SPI).

The Management Reserve covers internal risks (known unknowns) and is excluded from the cost baseline.

When cost reduction is necessary, project managers first target “easy-to-cut” items like quality assurance and control functions, risking long-term performance.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.16 Metrics

7.16.1 Project Management Information Systems (PMIS)
A PMIS contains all essential and supporting information for the project life cycle, including earned value measurement and significantly more metrics than just time and cost. The PMIS is often integrated into an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system, which helps with capacity planning by coordinating resource allocation across ongoing business activities and projects.


7.16.2 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)
A metric is a generic measurement, whereas a Key Performance Indicator (KPI) is specific, acting as an early warning sign that alerts management to potential issues.
Principles of KPIs:

KPIs are agreed upon beforehand and reflect the Critical Success Factors (CSFs) of the project.

KPIs are controllable, meaning they trigger changes necessary for corrective action.

A KPI metric is only “key” if it can make or break the project.

Characteristics of Effective KPIs:

Predictive: Able to forecast the future trend of performance.

Actionable: Triggers necessary changes or corrective action.

Relevant: Directly related to project success or failure.

Few in number: Focuses attention on necessary measurements (typically 6 to 10).

Aligned: Always aligned with corporate strategy and objectives.

The Pareto principle often suggests that 20% of the indicators will impact 80% of the project. David Parmenter suggests the 10/80/10 Rule for classification: 10 Results Indicators (RIs), 80 Performance Indicators (PIs), and 10 KPIs.


7.16.3 Value-Based Metrics
Modern projects focus on defining success based on the business value expected vs. business value delivered. A project is seen as an investment.

Value is a balance among performance, resource usage, and risk.

Measuring value requires going beyond “easy (soft/tangible) values” (like NPV or ROI calculators) to “hard (intangible) values” (like stockholder satisfaction, employee turnover, or corporate image) [523t].



7.16.4 Dashboards and Scorecards
Dashboards and scorecards are visual mechanisms for conveying critical information within a performance measurement system.

Dashboard: Monitors operational processes (e.g., project management KPIs, often updated intra-day or daily).

Scorecard: Charts the progress of tactical goals (e.g., long-term performance summaries, usually updated monthly/quarterly).

Three Types of Performance Dashboards [600, 600t]:

Operational: Monitors daily operations (Users: Supervisors, Specialists; Emphasis: Monitoring).

Tactical: Measures progress (Users: Managers, Analysts; Emphasis: Analysis).

Strategic: Monitors the execution of strategic objectives (Users: Executives, Managers; Emphasis: Management).


Chapter 15 Recap

The PMIS (often integrated with ERP) provides essential project and business information and aids in capacity planning.

KPIs are predictive, actionable, and critical measures used as early warning signs for project performance.

The 10/80/10 Rule provides guidance for limiting the number of truly critical KPIs.

Modern PM emphasizes metrics that capture business value and intangible assets (e.g., customer satisfaction, knowledge creation).

Dashboards focus on operational monitoring, while Scorecards track tactical progress toward goals, often cascading throughout the enterprise.

title: 7  Project Management
section: 7.17 Trade-Off Analysis in a Project Environment
text:
7.17 Trade-Off Analysis in a Project Environment

7.17.1 Trade-Off Definition
A trade-off is a situation where the project manager must sacrifice one project constraint (Time, Cost, or Performance) to maintain or satisfy another constraint.


7.17.2 Methodology for Trade-Off Analysis
Trade-off analysis is typically a six-step process:
Step 1: Determine the Objectives

Review original objectives and determine if they are still achievable.

Document the importance of the objectives to the company.

Step 2: Determine Current Status

Analyze WBS elements that are failing, identifying cause, impact, and proposed corrective action.

Evaluate whether performance, schedule, or cost is most suspect.

Step 3: Identify Alternatives

List all alternatives to eliminate the problem (e.g., changing specifications, reallocating resources, adjusting schedules).

Step 4: Analyze Alternatives

Determine the impact of each alternative on the three constraints (T, C, P).

Graphical analysis can be used by holding one constraint fixed:

Fixed Performance: Express cost as a function of time (Figure 16-5).

Fixed Cost: Plot performance versus time [617f].

Fixed Time: Plot cost versus performance [618f].


Step 5: Select Feasible Alternatives

Prepare a formal project update report for internal/external management, detailing alternative work scopes, schedules, and costs (e.g., minimum cost overrun, conformance to objectives, minimum schedule overrun).

Rank alternatives based on factors like customer impact, potential follow-on business, and loss of goodwill.

Step 6: Replanning the Project

Document the selected alternative in the revised project plan, SOW, and baseline documentation.



7.17.3 Impact of Contract Type
The final decision on trade-offs is often influenced by the type of contract.









Contract Type
1st to be Sacrificed
2nd to be Sacrificed
3rd to be Sacrificed




Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP)
Cost
Time
Performance


Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF)
Cost
Time
Performance


Cost-Plus-Incentive-Fee (CPIF)
Time
Performance
Cost


Cost-Sharing
Performance
Time
Cost



Note: On FFP and CPFF contracts, the contractor typically bears the most risk regarding cost, meaning cost is the first resource sacrificed when trade-offs are needed to meet time or performance requirements.

Chapter 16 Recap

Trade-off analysis is necessary when project deviation requires sacrificing one constraint (T, C, P) to meet others.

The methodology involves six steps, starting with clearly defining objectives and ending with formally replanning the project.

Graphical analysis helps visualize the impact of trade-offs when one constraint is held constant.

The sequence in which resources are sacrificed depends heavily on the contract type and who assumes the financial risk.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.18 Risk Management

7.18.1 Risk Defined
Risk is traditionally identified as a measure of the probability and consequence (impact) of not achieving a defined project goal. Risk involves uncertainty.

Risk Event: A future occurrence that is uncertain but can be planned for.

Risk can be categorized into business, legal, technical, organizational, and environmental risks.



7.18.2 Tolerance for Risk
The way risk is managed is based on the decision maker’s tolerance for risk. The three common classifications of risk tolerance are [636, 552f]:

Risk Averter (or Avoider): Prefers stability and certainty over high payoff risk.

Neutral Risk Taker: Focuses only on the expected monetary value.

Risk Taker (or Seeker): Prefers risk, enjoying the possibility of high payoff.



7.18.3 Decision-Making under Risk
When probabilities can be assigned to possible outcomes, Decision Trees can be used.
Three Steps to Construct a Decision Tree:

Build a logic tree from left to right, including decision points (box/square) and chance points (circle).

Put the probabilities of the states of nature on the branches.

Add the conditional payoffs to complete the tree.



7.18.4 Risk Management Process
Risk management is a continuous process that should be established early in the project life cycle. The process includes:

Plan Risk Management: Develop the Risk Management Plan (RMP).

Identify Risks: Determine the risks that may affect the project.

Perform Risk Analysis: Estimate probability and consequence (Qualitative/Quantitative).

Plan Risk Response: Develop options and actions to enhance opportunities and reduce threats.

Monitor and Control Risks: Track identified risks and implement plans.



7.18.5 Risk Analysis
Qualitative Risk Analysis estimates risk levels using probability-of-occurrence and consequence scales, mapped onto a risk matrix. Risks are classified as:

High Risk: Unacceptable, failure is highly likely. Requires immediate senior management attention and action.

Medium Risk: Some impact on cost/schedule/performance. Special action may be required.

Low Risk: Minimal impact. Normal management oversight is sufficient.

Quantitative Risk Analysis includes techniques like Expected Value (Expected Monetary Value), Decision Tree analysis, and Monte Carlo Simulation. Monte Carlo simulation uses random variables and probability distributions to model potential outcomes for cost and schedule risks.


7.18.6 Plan Risk Response
Risk Response Strategies are developed to handle threats and opportunities.








Response Option
Strategy for Threats (Negative Risks)
Strategy for Opportunities (Positive Risks)




Avoid/Exploit
Eliminate the threat by changing the project plan.
Ensure the opportunity happens and maximize its benefits.


Transfer/Share
Shift the consequence/risk ownership to a third party (e.g., insurance/contracting).
Partner with a third party to capitalize on the opportunity.


Mitigate/Enhance
Reduce the probability or impact of the threat.
Increase the probability or impact of the opportunity.


Accept
Take no action, accepting the risk if it occurs.
Take no action, benefiting if the opportunity occurs.




Chapter 17 Recap

Risk is measured by the probability and consequence of an event, and management must understand its tolerance for risk.

Decision Trees are tools used for complex decision-making under risk.

The formal risk management process requires planning, identification, qualitative/quantitative analysis (including Monte Carlo Simulation), response planning, and monitoring.

Risks are prioritized based on their level (High, Medium, Low).

There are four primary strategies for dealing with both threats (Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept) and opportunities (Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept).

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.19 Learning Curves

7.19.1 Learning Curves and the Experience Curve
The learning curve (or experience curve) theory states that as production doubles, the total manufacturing time and cost fall by a constant and predictable amount.

This effect extends across various industries (e.g., chemicals, electronics).

The experience curve effect shows that large market shares often lead to lower production costs due to economies of scale (fixed capital costs spread over more units).



7.19.2 Graphic Representation
When plotted on logarithmic paper, the labor-hour graph typically shows a straight line because the rate of change or decline in cost is a constant percentage of the prior cost as quantities double.

Slope of the Curve: A percentage figure representing the steepness and constant rate of improvement. An 80% learning curve means the cost/hours of unit two are 80% of unit one, unit four is 80% of unit two, etc..



7.19.3 Competitive Weapon
Learning curve analysis is a strong competitive weapon, particularly in pricing strategy.

By accurately predicting the average rate of improvement (cost reduction) over a large production run, a contractor can calculate a precise bid price based on the cumulative total hours saved.


Chapter 18 Recap

The Learning Curve (or Experience Curve) is an accurate estimating technique based on the principle that as cumulative production doubles, the unit cost or time decreases by a constant percentage.

This phenomenon results in economies of scale, leading to increased efficiency and lower unit costs for high production volumes.

The slope indicates the rate of improvement (e.g., an 80% slope).

Learning curves are essential tools in developing competitive pricing strategies for manufacturing projects.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.20 Contract Management

7.20.1 Introduction and Procurement Processes
Procurement refers to the acquisition of goods and services outside the organization. The contract management/procurement system includes four processes (from the buyer’s perspective):

Plan Procurements

Conduct Procurements

Administer Procurements

Close Procurements



7.20.2 Plan Procurements
Planning determines the necessary procurement actions.

Statement of Objectives (SOO): Used for performance-based projects, where the procuring organization describes only the end objectives. Contractors respond by proposing their own Statement of Work (SOW) detailing how they will achieve the objectives.

Statement of Work (SOW): Describes in specific detail what work needs to be performed.



7.20.3 Types of Contracts
Contract types determine the level of risk assumed by the buyer versus the seller (contractor).









Contract Type
Risk to Contractor
Risk to Buyer
Description




Fixed-Price (FP)or Lump-Sum
High
Low
The contractor assumes high risk; they must perform work at the negotiated value. Suitable for projects with well-defined scope.


Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee (CPFF)
Low
High
Buyer assumes the cost risk; the contractor is reimbursed for all allowable costs plus a fixed fee/profit. Used when scope is uncertain (e.g., R&D).


Cost-Plus-Incentive-Fee (CPIF)
Medium
Medium
Reimbursement for costs plus an incentive fee if performance targets (cost/schedule) are met or exceeded.


Fixed-Price-Incentive-Fee (FPIF)
Medium
Medium
Contractor assumes cost risk up to a ceiling price; shared savings or penalty is incurred if cost is below/above target.




Chapter 19 Recap

The procurement life cycle involves planning, conducting, administering, and closing procurements.

The Statement of Objectives (SOO) is used for performance-based contracts, requiring sellers to generate their own SOW.

The contract structure (Fixed-Price vs. Cost-Plus) determines how risk is shared between the buyer and seller.

Fixed-Price contracts put high cost risk on the contractor (suitable for defined scope), while Cost-Plus contracts shift cost risk to the buyer (suitable for uncertain R&D projects).

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.21 Quality Management

7.21.1 Definition of Quality
Modern quality management focuses on the customer. Quality is defined as those products and services perceived to meet or exceed the needs and expectations of the customer at a cost that represents outstanding value.

The shift is from “inspecting quality in” (detecting defects) to building quality into processes and deliverables(preventing defects).

The ultimate responsibility for quality management rests with the project manager.



7.21.2 The Cost of Quality (COQ)
The Cost of Quality (COQ) classifies quality costs into four categories:

Prevention Costs: Costs incurred to prevent failure (e.g., training, good planning, process reviews). These costs are expected to rise in effective quality systems.

Appraisal Costs: Costs incurred to measure or inspect quality (e.g., testing, audits). These costs are expected to decrease as processes improve.

Internal Failure Costs: Costs incurred when errors are found before delivery (e.g., rework, scrap, reengineering). This category provides the biggest potential savings.

External Failure Costs: Costs incurred when errors are found after delivery (e.g., warranty work, liability).



7.21.3 The Seven Quality Control Tools
The basic tools of Statistical Process Control (SPC) are used for data collection, pattern identification, and measuring variability.

Check Sheets/Data Tables: Used for systematic data collection (e.g., frequency of defects) [744, 745f].

Cause-and-Effect Analysis (Fishbone Diagrams): Used to identify the relationship between an effect (problem) and its causes (Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment, Personnel).

Systematic Method: Focuses analysis on one major cause category at a time.


Pareto Analysis: Identifies the “vital few” contributors that account for most quality problems (80/20 rule application).

Histograms: Graphical displays showing frequency distribution for variable data [748, 748f].

Scatter Diagrams: Shows the relationship between two variables [750, 750f].

Trend Analysis: Statistical method determining the equation that best fits data over time.

Control Charts: Statistical tools used to determine if a process is in control or if variation is due to special causes. Upper and Lower Control Limits (UCL/LCL) are typically set at \(\pm\) 3 standard deviations (3\(\sigma\)) from the mean.



7.21.4 Implementing Six Sigma
Six Sigma is a business strategy that uses statistical tools within a structured methodology to reduce defects and costs. It focuses on achieving intellectual capital and profit gains through disciplined application.

The methodology relies on the DMAIC structure :

Define: Define the problem and customer requirements.

Measure: Identify key measurements and metrics/KPIs.

Analyze: Analyze data using statistical tools to find the source of the problem.

Improve: Develop alternatives and implement improvements.

Control: Monitor and control the process to ensure improvements are sustained .



Chapter 20 Recap

Modern quality emphasizes customer focus and value, shifting from defect detection to prevention.

The Cost of Quality (COQ) is categorized into Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure costs.

Savings are maximized by increasing Prevention Costs to reduce failure costs.

The Seven Quality Control Tools are crucial for data collection and process analysis.

Pareto Analysis identifies the vital few problems, and Control Charts verify if a process is statistically stable (in control).

Six Sigma utilizes the DMAIC method to systematically improve processes and reduce variability .

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.22 Modern Developments in Project Management

7.22.1 The Project Management Maturity Model (PMMM)
The PMMM framework assesses an organization’s maturity level across five levels [635, 768f]:

Level 1: Common Language: Recognition of PM importance and the need for standardized terminology/knowledge.

Level 2: Common Processes: Definition and development of common processes so successes can be repeated.

Level 3: Singular Methodology: Development of an enterprise-wide project management methodology (EPM) that is tailorable and scalable .

Level 4: Benchmarking: Comparison of the organization’s practices against others .

Level 5: Continuous Improvement: Continuous optimization of the methodology based on benchmarking results .



7.22.2 Developing Effective Procedural Documentation
High project maturity is supported by procedural documentation based on forms, guidelines, templates, and checklists rather than rigid policies and procedures . This provides flexibility and standardization .


7.22.3 Capacity Planning
Capacity Planning ensures that sufficient human and nonhuman resources are available to support all selected projects and ongoing business activities . Excellent companies perform sophisticated capacity planning by :

Selecting projects based on strategic fit and profitability.

Defining objectives in business and technical terms.

Identifying constraints from summed schedules and plans.

Determining the objective of the plan (least cost, least time, or least risk).



7.22.4 Managing Multiple Projects
As organizations mature, there is a trend toward having one person manage multiple projects to reduce overmanagement costs . Success in this environment requires corporate changes in six major areas, including :

Prioritization: A credible system for prioritizing projects must be in effect.

Capacity Planning: Tight control on resource scheduling is essential .

Project Methodology: The methodology must be flexible (guidelines, checklists) rather than formal/rigid (policies, procedures) .



7.22.5 The Business of Scope Changes
While scope changes are inevitable, they should not be implemented if they drastically extend the payback period or risk exposure (e.g., liability/safety issues) . A common project failure cause is scope creep (features), where unnecessary functionality is added.

Chapter 21 Recap

The PMMM describes the path to project management maturity and excellence across five levels, starting with a Common Language and ending with Continuous Improvement .

Procedural documentation is most effective when built on flexible templates and checklists rather than rigid policies .

Capacity Planning is necessary to ensure human and technical resources are available to support the selected portfolio .

Managing multiple projects successfully requires high PM maturity, effective prioritization systems, and flexible methodologies .

Scope changes must be targeted for business value, carefully managed, and approved to prevent costly scope creep.

title: 7  Project Management
text:
7.23 Master Comprehensive Summary
Project Management (PM) is fundamentally a systems approach, integrating planning, organizing, directing, and controlling resources to achieve specific, short-term objectives. The modern imperative for PM is shifting from fulfilling the technical triple constraints (Time, Cost, Performance) to generating sustainable business value for customers and stakeholders.
Organizational success depends on achieving maturity (standardized processes and methodology) which leads to excellence (a continuous stream of successful projects). The methodology used must be flexible (a framework) to accommodate unique project characteristics, especially in complex or uncertain environments where Agile practices thrive over rigid Waterfall structures.
Core Frameworks and Methodologies:

Planning Foundation (WBS & Baselines): All planning is rooted in the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), which decomposes the project into measurable Work Packages and forms the basis for accountability, costing, and scheduling. The WBS leads to the establishment of the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB), integrating the approved Scope, Schedule, and Cost Baselines.

Scheduling (PERT/CPM): Network diagrams are critical for showing activity interdependencies. PERT is probabilistic, using three time estimates to calculate risk (\(\sigma\)), while CPM is deterministic. The process converts resource-unlimited proposals into resource-limited schedules by setting calendar dates.

Control (EVMS): The Earned Value Measurement System (EVMS) is the standard control framework, using BCWS (Planned Value), BCWP (Earned Value), and ACWP (Actual Cost) to calculate Cost and Schedule Variances (CV, SV) and efficiency trends (CPI, SPI).

Risk Management: Risk is defined by Probability \(\times\) Consequence. The process involves planning (RMP), identifying risks, performing qualitative/quantitative analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo Simulation), and selecting response strategies (Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept).

Quality Management: Quality is defined by the customer’s expectations and value perception. Focus is on increasing Prevention Costs to reduce Internal and External Failure Costs. Methodologies like Six Sigma (DMAIC) and the Seven Quality Control Tools (e.g., Pareto Analysis, Control Charts) are used to analyze processes and reduce variability.

Organizational Structure: The Matrix structure is commonly used to balance technical and project authority. The PMO provides governance, standardization, and knowledge management, acting as a center for continuous improvement.

The Role of the Project Manager:
The PM must function as a general manager and integrator. They require the skills defined in the Talent Triangle(Technical, Leadership, Strategic/Business). Because PMs often operate with limited formal authority, success hinges on interpersonal skills (communication, conflict resolution, negotiation) and utilizing non-formal power bases (Expert, Reward, Referent). They are also responsible for managing stakeholders, ensuring project alignment to the organizational portfolio, and tracking meaningful Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that predict success.

title: 8  PMBOK
text:
8.1 Executive Summary
The PMBOK Guide – Seventh Edition reflects the evolution of the project management profession driven by emerging technology, new approaches (like adaptive and hybrid), and rapid market changes. The foundational shift involves transitioning from a prescriptive, process-based approach to a principles-based standard focused on outcomes rather than solely on deliverables.
The core framework of this edition integrates The Standard for Project Management and the PMBOK® Guide. The Standard defines the 12 Project Management Principles which guide the behaviors and actions of project stakeholders. The PMBOK Guide presents eight Project Performance Domains that represent interactive, interrelated areas critical for delivering project outcomes.
Key takeaways from this new edition include:

title: 8  PMBOK
text:
Shift to Principles: Project management is guided by 12 principles that apply across all delivery approaches (predictive, adaptive, hybrid) and contexts.

Focus on Value and Outcomes: The primary measure of success is the realization of value and intended project outcomes, in addition to mere deliverables.

System for Value Delivery: Projects operate within a larger organizational context, often involving portfolios, programs, and operations, all linked in a system to advance organizational strategy.

Performance Domains: Project work is organized around eight performance domains (Stakeholders, Team, Development Approach and Life Cycle, Planning, Project Work, Delivery, Measurement, and Uncertainty) that function as an integrated system.

Tailoring: Project teams must deliberately adapt their approach, governance, and processes (tailoring) to suit the unique context, objectives, and complexity of each project.

New Resources: The edition expands on tools and techniques in a dedicated section on Models, Methods, and Artifacts. It also integrates with PMIstandards+™, an interactive digital platform for accessing supplemental content and emerging practices.

title: 8  PMBOK
section: 8.2 Part 1: The Standard for Project Management
text:
8.2 Part 1: The Standard for Project Management
The Standard for Project Management provides the basis for understanding project management and defines the system within which projects operate, including governance, functions, environment, and product management considerations. This standard is principles-based, focusing on generally accepted objectives for the core functions of project management practice.

8.2.1 Introduction
The standard identifies 12 project management principles that serve as foundational guidelines for project strategy, decision making, and problem solving. These principles are broadly based and internally consistent, offering wide parameters within which project teams can operate.

8.2.1.1 Key Terms and Concepts
The shift in the profession is reflected in several key terms:

Outcome: An end result or consequence of a process or project, focusing on the benefits and value delivered.

Deliverable: A unique and verifiable product, result, or capability required to complete a project.

Value: The worth, importance, or usefulness of something, perceived differently by various stakeholders (e.g., financial metrics, social benefits, or specific product features).

Project Management: Guiding project work to deliver intended outcomes using a broad range of approaches (predictive, hybrid, adaptive).

System for Value Delivery: A collection of strategic business activities (including portfolios, programs, projects, products, and operations) aimed at advancing an organization.

Project: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.




8.2.2 A System for Value Delivery
Projects exist within a larger organizational context (or “system”) to create value for stakeholders. This system aligns portfolios, programs, projects, products, and operations with the organization’s strategy.

8.2.2.1 Creating Value
Value is created through projects by producing new products or services, improving efficiency, enabling organizational change, or making positive social contributions. The components of the value delivery system—portfolios, programs, projects, products, and operations—work individually and collectively to create value.
Information Flow within the Value Delivery System: The system is most effective when information and feedback are consistently shared.

Senior Leadership Downstream (Black Arrows): Strategic information flows from senior leadership to portfolios. Portfolios share desired outcomes, benefits, and value with programs and projects. Deliverables, along with support and maintenance information, are passed from programs/projects to operations.

Operations Upstream (Light Gray Arrows): Information flows from operations (adjustments, fixes) to programs/projects. Programs/projects provide performance information and progress on achieving value to portfolios. Portfolios provide performance evaluations to senior leadership, who then assess overall strategy advancement.



8.2.2.2 Organizational Governance Systems
Governance systems operate alongside the value delivery system, providing a framework of functions and processes to manage issues, enable smooth workflows, and support decision making. Governance includes defining the authority to approve changes and aligning project governance with program and organizational governance.


8.2.2.3 Functions Associated with Projects
Project delivery is driven by people fulfilling necessary functions, which may be centralized (led by a designated project manager) or decentralized (project teams self-manage).
Key functions commonly associated with projects include:

Provide Oversight and Coordination: Orchestrating project work, leading planning/monitoring/controlling activities, consulting with leaders, and supporting benefits realization.

Present Objectives and Feedback: Providing insights and clear direction from customers (funding entity) and end users (direct consumers of the deliverable).

Facilitate and Support: Encouraging team collaboration, creating consensus, resolving conflicts, removing obstacles, and providing performance feedback.

Perform Work and Contribute Insights: Providing the knowledge, skills, and experience to produce products and realize outcomes.

Apply Expertise: Offering specific subject knowledge, vision, and expertise (internal or external) to support learning and work accuracy.

Provide Business Direction and Insight: Guiding project/product outcomes, prioritizing requirements based on business value and risk, and maximizing the value of deliverables.

Provide Resources and Direction (Sponsor Role): Promoting the project, securing decisions/resources/authority, and serving as a liaison between senior management and the team.

Maintain Governance: Establishing and sustaining policies and rules aligned with organizational strategy.



8.2.2.4 The Project Environment
Projects operate within internal and external environments that influence value delivery.

Internal Environment: Factors originating from within the organization (e.g., the organization itself, a program, or another project).

Examples: Process assets (tools, methodologies, PMO resources), governance documentation, data assets, knowledge assets (tacit knowledge), security/safety measures, organizational culture/structure, geographic distribution, and employee capability.


External Environment: Factors external to the organization.

Examples: Marketplace conditions (competitors, trends), social/cultural influences (customs, ethics), legal restrictions (regulations, labor laws), commercial databases, academic research, financial considerations, and the physical environment (working conditions).




8.2.2.5 Product Management Considerations
Product management involves integrating people, data, processes, and systems to create, maintain, and evolve a product throughout its product life cycle (introduction, growth, maturity, retirement). Projects often support product management by initiating work to create or enhance specific components or capabilities at any point in the product life cycle.
Forms of Product Management: Product management can exist in different forms relative to project structures:

Program Management within a Product Life Cycle: Used for large or long-running products where a set of programs and projects manage the various phases.

Project Management within a Product Life Cycle: Oversees the development and maturing of product capabilities as an ongoing business activity, chartering individual projects for enhancements.

Product Management within a Program: Applies the full product life cycle within a program’s boundaries to achieve specific benefits.




8.2.3 Project Management Principles
The 12 Project Management Principles guide the behavior of those involved in projects. They are nonprescriptive and allow for varied approaches while ensuring alignment with the project management values (Responsibility, Respect, Fairness, and Honesty).
The principles guide project management activity across all delivery approaches (predictive, adaptive, hybrid).

8.2.3.1 Be a Diligent, Respectful, and Caring Steward
Stewardship involves acting responsibly, with integrity, care, and trustworthiness, while maintaining compliance with guidelines. Stewards demonstrate a broad commitment to the financial, social, and environmental impacts of the projects they support.
Key Aspects of Stewardship:

Integrity: Behaving honestly and ethically, serving as a role model, and challenging others to consider their words and actions.

Care: Diligently overseeing organizational matters (finances, resources), understanding the environmental/social consequences of project outcomes, and creating a transparent working environment.

Trustworthiness: Representing oneself and one’s authority accurately, and proactively identifying and protecting the project from conflicts of interest.

Compliance: Adhering to all applicable laws, rules, regulations, and requirements.



8.2.3.2 Create a Collaborative Project Team Environment
Projects are delivered by teams that work collaboratively to accomplish shared objectives more effectively than individuals working alone. A collaborative environment facilitates learning, development, and optimal contributions.
Elements of a Collaborative Environment:

Team Agreements: Behavioral parameters and working norms established by the team at the project start and evolved over time.

Organizational Structures: Tailored arrangements of project work elements and processes (based on roles, functions, or authority).

Clear Roles: Defining authority (the right to make decisions), accountability (being answerable for an outcome, which is not shared), and responsibility (the obligation to fulfill something, which can be shared).

Diversity and Respect: Leveraging different perspectives and managing conflict productively.



8.2.3.3 Effectively Engage with Stakeholders
Stakeholders are individuals, groups, or organizations that may affect, be affected by, or perceive themselves to be affected by a project’s outcome. Proactively engaging stakeholders throughout the project advances value delivery.
Impact of Stakeholder Engagement: Effective engagement can influence nearly every aspect of the project:

Scope/requirements

Schedule and Cost

Project team management

Risk tolerance

Quality requirements

Success factors and benefits realization

Engagement relies heavily on interpersonal skills (e.g., initiative, honesty, respect, empathy) to build solid relationships through frequent, two-way communication, fostering shared understanding and collective solutions.


8.2.3.4 Focus on Value
Project teams must continually evaluate and adjust project alignment to business objectives and intended benefits. Value is the ultimate indicator of project success. Value can be defined qualitatively and/or quantitatively and may be realized throughout, at the end of, or after the project.
Value is derived from:

Business Case: The rationale for the project, providing details on strategic alignment, risk exposure, and economic feasibility. A business case contains the business need, project justification (why the need is worth the investment), and business strategy.

Outcomes vs. Deliverables: Focusing on outcomes—what the customer intended to achieve (e.g., productivity)—rather than just the outputs (e.g., software solution).

Continuous Evaluation: Teams continuously evaluate progress against desired outputs and the business case to confirm alignment. If alignment is lost, the organization may choose to terminate the effort.



8.2.3.5 Recognize, Evaluate, and Respond to System Interactions
A project is a system of interdependent and interacting domains of activity that exists within other larger systems (a system of systems, e.g., portfolios and programs). Systems thinking requires taking a holistic view of how project parts interact with each other and with external systems.
Practicing Systems Thinking:

Project teams should constantly attend to internal and external conditions, navigating changes to keep the project in agreement with stakeholders.

Empathy, critical thinking with a “big picture” focus, and challenging assumptions are crucial skills.

The team must adjust to the changing needs of end users, sponsors, or customers.

Decisions should benefit the organization as a whole, not just one part of the project.



8.2.3.6 Demonstrate Leadership Behaviors
Effective leadership promotes project success and positive outcomes. Leadership is distinctly different from authority(the positional right to exercise power, often formally delegated).
Key Leadership Behaviors:

Shared Leadership: Any project team member, sponsor, or stakeholder can demonstrate leadership behaviors, contributing influence, creativity, and motivation.

Adaptability: Effective leaders adapt their style (e.g., autocratic, democratic, servant leadership) to the specific situation. For instance, in chaos, a directive style may be needed; with competent staff, empowered delegation may yield higher productivity.

Role Modeling: Leaders must demonstrate desired behavior in areas of honesty, integrity, and ethical conduct.

Motivation: Leaders understand and leverage what motivates people (e.g., finance, autonomy, purpose, growth opportunity) to foster shared responsibility and a healthy environment.



8.2.3.7 Tailor Based on Context
Since each project is unique, project success requires adapting the approach to the unique context. Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of the approach, governance, and processes to make them suitable for the environment and the work at hand.
Goals of Tailoring:

Maximize value, manage constraints, and improve performance.

Use “just enough” processes, methods, templates, and artifacts to achieve the desired outcome.

Produce outcomes like deeper commitment from team members, reduced waste, and more efficient use of resources.

The tailoring process is iterative and continuous throughout the project life cycle.


8.2.3.8 Build Quality into Processes and Deliverables
Quality is the degree to which inherent characteristics fulfill requirements, satisfying stakeholder expectations. Quality focuses on conformance to acceptance criteria and ensuring fitness for use.
Dimensions of Quality: Quality activities aim to minimize waste and maximize the probability of attaining the desired outcome by focusing on prevention and early defect detection. Dimensions include:

Performance (functionality)

Conformity (fitness for use/meeting specifications)

Reliability (consistent metrics)

Efficiency (greatest output with least input/effort)

Satisfaction (positive end-user feedback)

Quality management practices ensure that deliverables meet acceptance criteria and business objectives, reducing rework, scrap, and complaints.


8.2.3.9 Navigate Complexity
Complexity is a characteristic of a project or its environment that is difficult to manage due to human behavior, system behavior, uncertainty, and ambiguity. Complexity can emerge at any point during the project.
Sources of Complexity:

Human Behavior: Subjectivity, conflicting personal agendas, diverse cultural norms, and geographic distribution among stakeholders.

System Behavior: Dynamic interdependencies among project elements causing unforeseen issues and unclear cause-and-effect relationships.

Technological Innovation: Disruption caused by new technology and the uncertainty of how it will be used.

Ambiguity and Uncertainty: Blurring causal relationships where impacts are ill-defined.

Project teams must continuously evaluate and navigate complexity using methods like systems thinking, past experience, and continuous learning.


8.2.3.10 Optimize Risk Responses
A risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive (opportunity) or negative (threat) effect on objectives. Project teams continuously identify and evaluate known and emergent risks throughout the life cycle.
Risk Optimization Goals:

Maximize positive risks (opportunities) and decrease exposure to negative risks (threats).

Manage the overall project risk (the effect of uncertainty on the project as a whole) to keep exposure within an acceptable range, influenced by organizational risk attitude, appetite, and threshold.

Effective Risk Responses must be appropriate for the risk’s significance, cost effective, realistic, agreed to by stakeholders, and owned by a responsible person.


8.2.3.11 Embrace Adaptability and Resiliency
Projects rarely perform exactly as initially planned. Adaptability is the ability to respond to changing conditions, while resiliency is the ability to absorb impacts and recover quickly from setbacks or failures.
Capabilities Supporting Adaptability and Resiliency:

Using short feedback loops and continuous learning.

Employing diverse, skilled project teams.

Designing processes that balance work velocity and requirement stability.

Envisioning outcomes (rather than fixed deliverables) to enable alternative, stronger solutions.

Deferring decision making to the last responsible moment.



8.2.3.12 Enable Change to Achieve the Envisioned Future State
Projects are agents of change, moving an organization from a current state to a future desired state. Change management(or enablement) is the comprehensive, structured approach for transitioning individuals, groups, and organizations to realize desired benefits. (This is distinct from project change control, which modifies project baselines).
Enabling Change:

Change can originate internally (performance gap) or externally (technological advances, pressures).

The project team works to address resistance and fatigue by communicating the vision and goals early to achieve buy-in.

The speed of change must be adapted to the stakeholder’s appetite and ability to assimilate it; attempting too much change too quickly leads to resistance or saturation.

title: 8  PMBOK
section: 8.3 Part 2: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK® Guide)
text:
8.3 Part 2: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK® Guide)
The PMBOK Guide expands on the concepts in The Standard, detailing the application of principles within the context of eight Project Performance Domains.

8.3.1 Introduction (PMBOK® Guide)

8.3.1.1 Structure of the PMBOK® Guide
The PMBOK® Guide – Seventh Edition is structured into three main sections:

Project Performance Domains: Describes eight areas critical for effective delivery.

Tailoring: Explains what tailoring is and how to apply it to individual projects.

Models, Methods, and Artifacts: Presents commonly used options for performing project work.



8.3.1.2 Relationship of the PMBOK® Guide and The Standard for Project Management
The 12 Project Management Principles guide behavior, while the eight Project Performance Domains represent broad areas of focus where that behavior is demonstrated. They are conceptually overlapped and work in unison.



8.3.2 Project Performance Domains
A Project Performance Domain is a group of related activities critical for effectively delivering project outcomes. These domains are interactive, interrelated, and interdependent, operating concurrently throughout the project. The activities undertaken within each domain are determined by the unique context of the organization, project, deliverables, and team.

8.3.2.1 Stakeholder Performance Domain
This domain focuses on activities related to maintaining alignment with stakeholders and engaging them proactively to foster positive relationships and satisfaction.
Desired Outcomes:

A productive working relationship with stakeholders throughout the project.

Stakeholder agreement with project objectives.

Project beneficiaries are supportive and satisfied, and opponents do not negatively impact results.

Steps for Effective Stakeholder Engagement (Identify-Understand-Analyze-Prioritize-Engage-Monitor):

Identify: Continuously identify high-level and detailed stakeholders.

Understand and Analyze: Seek to understand stakeholders’ feelings, beliefs, values, power, impact, attitude, and interests. Analyze how stakeholders interact with each other.

Prioritize: Focus engagement efforts, often concentrating on stakeholders with the most power and interest.

Engage: Work collaboratively to elicit requirements, manage expectations, resolve issues, and make decisions, using soft skills and leadership.

Communication Methods: Push (one-way, e.g., emails), Pull (information sought by stakeholders, e.g., intranet search), and Interactive (two-way exchange, e.g., meetings).


Monitor: Continuously assess whether the current engagement strategy is effective and whether stakeholder satisfaction levels are being met.



8.3.2.2 Team Performance Domain
This domain focuses on establishing the culture and environment needed to turn diverse individuals into a high-performing project team, encouraging leadership behaviors from all members.
Desired Outcomes:

Shared ownership of outcomes.

A high-performing team.

Applicable leadership and interpersonal skills demonstrated by all team members.

Key Concepts:

Management vs. Leadership: Management focuses on the means (processes, planning, monitoring), while leadership focuses on people (influencing, motivating, listening).

Servant Leadership: A style focusing on addressing the needs and development of team members to enable high performance. Behaviors include obstacle removal, shielding the team from diversions, and providing encouragement.

Project Team Culture: The team’s norms and ways of working, established deliberately or informally. Project managers foster a safe, respectful environment by modeling transparency, integrity, respect, and courage.

Leadership Skills: Critical thinking, motivation (intrinsic vs. extrinsic), and interpersonal skills (e.g., emotional intelligence, conflict management, decision making).

Breakdown of Emotional Intelligence (EI): EI is critical for effective communication and collaboration.







Aspect
Description




Self-Awareness
Ability to conduct a realistic self-assessment of one’s own emotions, strengths, and weaknesses.


Self-Management
Ability to control and redirect disruptive feelings and impulses; thinking before acting.


Social Awareness
Empathy, understanding others’ feelings, and reading nonverbal cues.


Social Skill
Managing groups, building social networks, finding common ground, and building rapport.





8.3.2.3 Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
This domain addresses establishing the development approach, delivery cadence, and project life cycle necessary to optimize project outcomes. The type of deliverable drives the development approach, which in turn influences the delivery cadence and the resulting project life cycle phases.
Delivery Cadence (Timing and Frequency):

Single Delivery: Deliverable provided only at the end of the project (e.g., process reengineering).

Multiple Deliveries: Components delivered at different times throughout the project, often sequentially or separately (e.g., new drug development phases).

Periodic Deliveries: Deliveries on a fixed schedule (e.g., monthly releases).

Continuous Delivery: Feature increments delivered immediately to customers using small batches of work (often for digital products).

Development Approaches (Methods used to create the product):








Approach
Description
Applicability




Predictive (Waterfall)
Requirements, scope, schedule, and cost are defined early and are relatively stable.
Useful when scope is well-understood, risk is high (requiring frequent review), or regulations mandate up-front planning.


Hybrid
Combination of predictive and adaptive elements.
Useful when requirements uncertainty is mixed, or when deliverables can be modularized (e.g., adaptive for product development, predictive for deployment).


Adaptive (Agile)
Requirements subject to high uncertainty and volatility; initial known requirements are refined based on user feedback.
Useful when requirements are uncertain, volatile, or complex, emphasizing short iterations and high stakeholder involvement.



Development Cycles/Methods:

Iterative: Useful for clarifying requirements and investigating options; produces sufficient capability prior to final iteration.

Incremental: Used to produce a deliverable throughout a series of iterations; each iteration adds functionality within a timebox until the final completion.

Project Life Cycle Phases: Phases can be sequential (predictive, Figure 2-9) or overlapping/repeating (incremental/adaptive, Figures 2-10, 2-11). Phases often conclude with a Phase Gate Review to check if desired outcomes or exit criteria have been achieved.


8.3.2.4 Planning Performance Domain
Planning involves organizing, elaborating, and coordinating project work initially, ongoing, and as the project evolves. The time spent planning must be appropriate for the situation (“just enough”).
Desired Outcomes:

The project progresses in an organized, coordinated, and deliberate manner.

Evolving information is elaborated to produce deliverables.

Planning information is sufficient to manage stakeholder expectations.

Planning Variables:

Development Approach: Dictates how much planning is done up front versus iteratively (e.g., rolling wave planning in predictive, iteration planning in adaptive).

Estimating: Developing quantitative assessments of work effort, duration, costs, and resources. Estimates evolve from broad ranges (low accuracy) to narrow ranges (high accuracy) as the project progresses.

Schedules: Modeling project activities, durations, and dependencies.

Predictive Scheduling: Decompose scope, sequence activities, estimate resources, and adjust. Techniques include Crashing (shortening duration by adding resources) and Fast Tracking (performing sequential activities in parallel).

Adaptive Scheduling: Uses incremental planning based on iterations and releases, prioritizing work via a backlog.


Budget: Evolving from cost estimates aggregated into a cost baseline. Includes Contingency Reserve (for identified risks) and Management Reserve (for unexpected in-scope work).

Procurement: Planning involves conducting a make-or-buy analysis and providing advance information to contracting professionals.



8.3.2.5 Project Work Performance Domain
This domain ensures efficient and effective project performance by establishing processes, managing physical resources, and fostering a learning environment.
Desired Outcomes:

Efficient and effective project performance.

Appropriate project processes (e.g., continuous improvement, lean production).

Effective management of physical resources and procurements.

Key Concepts/Activities:

Project Processes: Establishing, reviewing, and optimizing processes using techniques like Lean production(removing waste/non-value-adding activities) and process auditing.

Balancing Competing Constraints: Continuously managing trade-offs among constraints (scope, schedule, budget, quality) to maintain stakeholder satisfaction.

Procurement Work: Working with contracting officers to develop bid documents (RFI, RFP, RFQ), conducting bidder conferences, selecting vendors, and negotiating contracts (Fixed-Price, Cost-Reimbursable, T&M).

Monitoring New Work and Changes: In adaptive projects, managing backlog priority to handle evolving work; in predictive projects, rigorously using the Change Control Process.

Learning Throughout the Project: Developing and sharing explicit knowledge (codified data, like manuals) and tacit knowledge (personal experience, shared via networking or job shadowing).



8.3.2.6 Delivery Performance Domain
Delivery focuses on meeting requirements, scope, and quality expectations to produce the expected deliverables that will drive the intended outcomes.
Desired Outcomes:

Projects contribute to business objectives and strategy.

Projects realize the outcomes and benefits they were initiated to deliver.

Key Concepts/Activities:

Requirements Management: Eliciting, documenting (clear, concise, verifiable), and managing requirements (often by a Product Owner/Business Analyst). In uncertain environments, requirements may be discovered using prototypes or demonstrations.

Scope Definition: Defining the sum of products, services, and results. Scope is decomposed using a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) (predictive) or themes/epics/features/user stories (adaptive).

Moving Targets of Completion: Managing “Done Drift” (the project goal moving due to new features/competitors, Figure 2-21) in volatile markets and mitigating “Scope Creep” (uncontrolled scope expansion) in stable environments via formal change control.

Quality and Cost of Quality (COQ): Quality focuses on required performance levels. COQ analyzes costs related to prevention (investing early) and appraisal (checking conformance) versus costs related to internal failure(scrap, rework) and external failure (defects found by the customer).

Proactive quality work minimizes costs, countering the principle of the Cost of Change Curve (Figure 2-22), where the cost to fix defects increases dramatically later in the life cycle.




8.3.2.7 Measurement Performance Domain
Measurement involves assessing project performance and implementing appropriate responses to maintain optimal performance. Effective measures provide actionable, timely data to facilitate informed decision making.
Desired Outcomes:

A reliable understanding of project status.

Timely and appropriate actions to keep performance on track.

Key Measures and Metrics:

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Quantifiable measures of success. They include leading indicators (predict changes/trends, e.g., lack of risk process) and lagging indicators (measure past performance, e.g., project status).

SMART Metrics: Effective metrics are Specific, Meaningful (or Measurable), Achievable (or Agreed to), Relevant (or Realistic/Reasonable), and Timely (or Time bound).

Baseline Performance: Comparison of actual results against the approved baseline (Cost, Schedule, Scope). This uses Earned Value Analysis (EVA) metrics (e.g., CV, SV, CPI, SPI) and forecasting metrics (e.g., EAC, ETC, VAC).

Delivery Metrics: Used primarily in adaptive approaches, including Work in Progress (WIP), Lead Time, Cycle Time, and Throughput (items completed in a fixed time).

Stakeholder Metrics: Measuring satisfaction (e.g., Net Promoter Score® (NPS®)).

Presenting Information:

Dashboards: Electronic visual summaries of metrics, often using charts and graphs (RAG charts, bar charts).

Information Radiators (Big Visible Charts): Visible, physical displays providing timely knowledge sharing (often manually maintained, e.g., risk logs, burn charts).

Visual Controls: Charts illustrating processes to compare actual against expected performance (e.g., Task Boards/Kanban Boards to limit WIP, Burn Charts to track velocity).

Measurement Pitfalls: Teams must avoid the Hawthorne Effect (behavior influenced by measurement), using Vanity Metrics (data without useful decision-making information), and Demoralization from unachievable goals.


8.3.2.8 Uncertainty Performance Domain
This domain focuses on exploring, assessing, and responding to the threats and opportunities presented by uncertainty, which is inherent in all projects.
Key Concepts:

Uncertainty: Lack of understanding/awareness of issues, events, paths, or solutions. Reduced by gathering information, preparing for multiple outcomes, set-based design, and building resilience.

Ambiguity: A state of being unclear, having difficulty identifying causes, or having multiple options. Solutions include progressive elaboration, experimentation, and prototyping.

Complexity: Difficult to manage due to human behavior, system behavior, and ambiguity. Handled via decoupling systems, simulation, diversity in perspectives (reframing), and process-based approaches (iterate, engage, fail safe).

Volatility: Rapid and unpredictable change, typically impacting cost and schedule. Addressed via alternatives analysis and using reserves (cost or schedule).

Risk (Threats and Opportunities): Project teams proactively identify risks aligned with the organization’s Risk Appetite and Risk Thresholds.

Risk Response Strategies:








Strategy
Threats (Negative Risks)
Opportunities (Positive Risks)




Avoid/Exploit
Eliminate the threat or protect the project from impact.
Act to ensure the opportunity occurs.


Mitigate/Enhance
Decrease probability/impact of the threat.
Increase probability/impact of the opportunity.


Transfer/Share
Shift impact/ownership to a third party (e.g., insurance, warranty).
Allocate ownership to a third party best able to capture the benefit.


Accept
Acknowledge the risk and take no action unless it occurs.
Acknowledge the opportunity but take no proactive steps.


Escalate
Threat/opportunity is outside project scope or manager’s authority.







8.3.3 Tailoring
Tailoring is essential because no single project management approach works for every project. It ensures that the approach, governance, and processes are suitable for the given environment.

8.3.3.1 Why Tailor?
Tailoring ensures appropriate management rigor based on project criticality, size, duration, complexity, industry, and organizational maturity. Benefits include greater team commitment, reduced waste, efficient resource use, and a customer-oriented focus.


8.3.3.2 What to Tailor
Project teams can tailor the following aspects:

Life Cycle and Development Approach Selection: Choosing between predictive, iterative, incremental, or hybrid approaches.

Processes: Adding rigor, modifying to suit team needs, removing wasteful steps, blending practices (e.g., mixing methods), and aligning differing elements for consistency.

Engagement: Deciding on the appropriate people involvement, the level of empowerment given to the team, and how integration occurs across different contributing entities.

Tools: Selecting the appropriate software or equipment.

Methods and Artifacts: Choosing the means to achieve outcomes and the necessary documentation and templates.



8.3.3.3 The Tailoring Process
The tailoring process involves four iterative steps, beginning after the project environment has been analyzed.
Step-by-Step Breakdown of the Tailoring Process:

Select Initial Development Approach: Determine the most appropriate approach (predictive, hybrid, or adaptive) based on knowledge of the product, delivery cadence, and organizational constraints. Tools like a suitability filter can assist by assessing criteria related to culture, team, and project factors.

Tailor for the Organization: Adjust the initial approach to align with the organization’s established methodology, governance policies, and strategic/stewardship goals. This step may involve oversight and approval by the PMO or VDO (Value Delivery Office).

Tailor for the Project: Adjust the approach based on specific project attributes, such as product/deliverable complexity, project team size/geography/experience, and organizational culture.

Implement Ongoing Improvement: Since tailoring is iterative, regularly conduct reviews (e.g., phase gates, retrospectives) to inspect and adapt the process, ensuring continuous learning and improvement.



8.3.3.4 Tailoring the Performance Domains
The work within each of the eight performance domains can be tailored based on project uniqueness.

Stakeholders: Tailor based on stakeholder location (colocated/distributed), language diversity, number of stakeholders, and complexity of stakeholder networks.

Project Team: Tailor based on geographical location, cultural perspectives, presence of special needs, and team maturity/training needs.

Development Approach and Life Cycle: Tailor the development approach (iterative, incremental, hybrid) and define the necessary project phases and exit criteria.

Planning: Tailor based on environmental factors, budget constraints, correlation between resources and productivity, and the complexity of procurements.

Project Work: Tailor the management processes, knowledge management strategy (explicit vs. tacit), and information collection/storage technology.

Delivery: Tailor requirements management, validation/control policies, quality standards (industry/regulatory), and approaches for handling unstable requirements.

Uncertainty: Tailor based on risk appetite, risk tolerance, project size/duration/complexity, level of innovation, and the strategic importance of the project.

Measurement: Tailor how value is measured (financial vs. nonfinancial), required status reporting frequency, and how data capture supports benefits realization after closure.




8.3.4 Models, Methods, and Artifacts
This section provides a non-exhaustive, non-prescriptive array of common resources useful in managing projects.

Model: A thinking strategy used to explain a process, framework, or phenomenon.

Method: The means for achieving an outcome, output, result, or project deliverable.

Artifact: A template, document, output, or project deliverable.

Project teams should avoid using any element that duplicates effort, provides misleading information, or is not useful to stakeholders.

8.3.4.1 Commonly Used Models








Model Category
Key Model Examples
Core Concept Explained (Plain Language)




Situational Leadership
Situational Leadership® II, OSCAR Model
Leadership style must adapt to the project team member’s competence and commitment level (Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating).


Communication
Cross-cultural Communication, Effectiveness of Communication Channels, Gulf of Execution and Evaluation
Explains how filters (culture, experience, language) distort messages and how communication effectiveness depends on the medium’s “richness” (complex info needs richer channels).


Motivation
Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Motivation, Theory of Needs (Achievement, Power, Affiliation), Theory X/Y/Z
Identifies that intrinsic factors (autonomy, mastery, purpose) are often more effective for complex work than extrinsic rewards.


Change
ADKAR® Model, Kotter’s 8-Step Process for Leading Change, Virginia Satir Change Model
Provides frameworks for structuring the transition from a current state to a future state, focusing on urgency, coalition building, vision, and reinforcement.


Complexity
Cynefin Framework, Stacey Matrix
Classifies decision-making contexts (Obvious, Complicated, Complex, Chaotic) based on cause-and-effect clarity and uncertainty of requirements/technology.


Project Team Development
Tuckman Ladder (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing), Drexler/Sibbet Model (Orientation, Trust, Goals, Commitment)
Describes the predictable stages a team progresses through to achieve high performance.


Other
Conflict Model, Negotiation Models (Win-Win), Planning Model (Optimal Planning)
Provides strategies for addressing conflict (e.g., competing, collaborating, compromising), negotiating outcomes, and identifying the “sweet spot” for planning effort.





8.3.4.2 Commonly Used Methods
Methods are means for achieving outcomes, often grouped by purpose.








Method Category
Key Method Examples
Core Application (Plain Language)




Data Gathering & Analysis
Alternatives Analysis, Benchmarking, Cost-Benefit Analysis, Earned Value Analysis (EVA), Root Cause Analysis (RCA), Simulation (Monte Carlo), SWOT Analysis
Used to collect, assess, and evaluate data to gain a deeper understanding or justify decisions (e.g., checking project performance against baselines, identifying underlying causes of variance).


Estimating
Analogous, Parametric, Multipoint, Relative Estimating (Story Points), Wideband Delphi
Techniques used to approximate project work, duration, or cost based on historical data, algorithms, or comparison against similar work.


Meetings & Events
Backlog Refinement, Bidder Conference, Change Control Board (CCB), Daily Standup, Iteration Review, Kickoff, Lessons Learned
Structured gatherings for communication, collaboration, planning, reviewing progress, managing change, and knowledge sharing.


Other
Impact Mapping, Modeling (Prototyping), Net Promoter Score® (NPS®), Prioritization Schema (MoSCoW), Timebox
Strategic and tactical tools for high-level roadmaps, testing designs, gauging customer loyalty, ordering work, and limiting work duration.





8.3.4.3 Commonly Used Artifacts
Artifacts are templates, documents, or deliverables used to record, plan, and communicate project information.








Artifact Category
Key Artifact Examples
Core Purpose (Plain Language)




Strategy
Business Case, Project Charter, Project Vision Statement, Roadmap
