High-level documents created at the start to authorize the project and define its purpose, goals, and strategic alignment.


Logs & Registers
Assumption Log, Backlog, Change Log, Issue Log, Risk Register, Stakeholder Register
Used to continuously record and monitor evolving project aspects and decisions throughout the project lifecycle.


Plans
Project Management Plan (overall), Communications Management Plan, Quality Management Plan, Risk Management Plan, Stakeholder Engagement Plan
Documents describing how specific project aspects (e.g., cost, scope, communication) will be executed, monitored, and controlled.


Hierarchy Charts
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS), Product Breakdown Structure
Hierarchical decomposition of high-level information into greater detail (e.g., breaking scope into manageable work packages).


Baselines
Budget, Project Schedule, Scope Baseline, Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)
The approved version of plans used as a fixed point for comparison against actual performance to measure variances.


Visual Data & Info
Dashboards, Gantt Charts, Histogram, Kanban/Task Boards, Velocity Charts, S-Curves, Traceability Matrices
Charts and diagrams that organize and present analyzed data visually to aid in decision-making and project status understanding.


Reports
Quality Report, Risk Report, Status Report
Formal summaries of information communicated to stakeholders, sponsors, or PMOs.


Agreements
Fixed-Price Contracts (FFP), Cost-Reimbursable Contracts (CPFF), Time and Materials (T&M)
Mutually binding documents defining the intentions, scope, and payment obligations between parties.

title: 8  PMBOK
text:
8.4 Final Comprehensive Summary
The PMBOK® Guide – Seventh Edition represents a substantial evolution in project management thinking, shifting the focus from rigidly defined processes to adaptability, value, and outcomes. The core framework consists of The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge.
The foundation of the practice rests on 12 Project Management Principles (e.g., Stewardship, Focus on Value, Tailoring, Adaptability, Optimize Risk) that guide professional behavior regardless of the development approach used. This principles-based approach is supported by a holistic view of the organization as a System for Value Delivery, where projects are integral components enabling strategic objectives.
The application framework is built around eight Project Performance Domains (Stakeholders, Team, Development Approach and Life Cycle, Planning, Project Work, Delivery, Measurement, and Uncertainty). These domains are interconnected areas of focus that run concurrently throughout the project to ensure effective delivery. Success within each domain relies heavily on tailoring the project approach, governance, and processes to fit the unique context, rather than applying a single, generic methodology.
Project teams must actively manage uncertainty (risk, complexity, ambiguity, and volatility) and ensure continuous improvement and learning. The execution of project work is supported by a wide array of situational Models, Methods, and Artifacts—tools selected and refined via tailoring to maximize efficiency, quality, and value realization. Finally, this edition links users to PMIstandards+™ to provide access to dynamic, supplemental content reflecting the rapidly changing nature of the project environment.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.1 Executive Summary
This briefing document summarizes key themes and ideas from the provided sources on marketing principles, customer value, market analysis, and buyer behavior. It highlights the importance of a customer-centric approach, the strategic process of defining marketing goals, understanding the dynamic marketing environment, and the intricate nature of consumer and business buying decisions. The document emphasizes that successful marketing is not merely about selling products, but about creating, communicating, and delivering superior customer value and building lasting relationships.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.2 Defining Marketing and the Marketing Process
text:
9.2 Defining Marketing and the Marketing Process
Marketing is fundamentally “the set of strategies and activities by which companies acquire and engage customers, build strong customer relationships, and create superior customer value in order to capture value from customers in return.”
An Expanded Model of the Marketing Process p.52

9.2.1 The marketing process involves five core steps:

Understanding the marketplace and customer needs and wants. This is the foundational step, requiring deep insight into what customers truly desire. Marketers differentiate between needs (felt deprivation), wants (needs shaped by culture and personality), and demands (wants backed by buying power).

Designing a customer value-driven marketing strategy. This involves identifying target customers and developing a value proposition – “the set of benefits or values it promises to deliver to consumers to satisfy their needs.”

Constructing an integrated marketing mix (the Four Ps) [in strategy books they change this into 4 Ms]. This includes Product (offerings, services, experiences), Price (cost to obtain the product), Place (making the product available), and Promotion (communicating benefits and persuading purchase).

Engaging customers, building profitable customer relationships, and creating customer delight. This involves active customer relationship management (CRM) and fostering deep engagement.

Capturing value from customers in return to achieve profits and customer equity.


9.2.1.1 Marketing Orientations
Five alternative concepts guide marketing strategies:

Production Concept: Focuses on availability and affordability. Risk of “marketing myopia” by focusing too narrowly on operations rather than customer needs.

Product Concept: Focuses on quality, performance, and innovative features. Also risks marketing myopia by assuming a superior product will sell itself without proper marketing.

Selling Concept: Emphasizes large-scale selling and promotion, typically for “unsought goods.” Carries high risks as it prioritizes sales transactions over long-term customer relationships, often selling “what the company makes rather than to make what the market wants.”

Marketing Concept: Customer-centered, focusing on “knowing the needs and wants of target markets and delivering the desired satisfactions better than competitors do.” It’s a “sense-and-respond philosophy.”

Societal Marketing Concept: Considers consumers’ wants, the company’s requirements, consumers’ long-run interests, and society’s long-run interests, balancing “company profits, consumer wants, and society’s interests.” This concept aligns with “shared value,” creating economic value that also creates value for society.



9.2.1.2 CRM

Definition and Importance: Customer satisfaction is achieved when a product’s performance matches or surpasses customer expectations. It is crucial for fostering repeat business, loyalty, and positive word-of-mouth.

Achieving Satisfaction: This involves setting realistic expectations and then exceeding them through exceptional value and service.

Role in CRM: Satisfaction is a fundamental building block for CRM, the overall process of building and maintaining profitable customer relationships.

Leveraging Technology: Companies use CRM systems, AI, and big data to gain deep customer insights, personalize experiences, and engage with customers in real-time.

Ultimate Goal: The primary objective is to cultivate customer loyalty, thereby increasing customer lifetime value (the total amount a customer spends over their lifetime) and customer equity (the total combined lifetime values of all customers).

Challenges: Key challenges include balancing customer satisfaction with profitability and responsibly managing customer data to ensure privacy and trust.


9.2.1.2.1 Building Customer and Partner Relationships in CRM

Varying Levels of Relationships: Companies tailor the depth of their customer relationships based on customer value. This ranges from basic relationships with a broad customer base (e.g., through advertising and social media) to full partnerships with key, high-margin clients involving direct, personal interaction.

Partner Relationship Management: Effective CRM requires collaboration beyond the customer. This involves working closely with internal partners (all company departments aligned to be customer-focused) and external partners (like suppliers and channel distributors) to create a superior value delivery network.

Tools for Strengthening Bonds: Marketers use specific tools to build stronger connections, most notably frequency marketing and loyalty programs that reward customers for repeat business, such as airline frequent-flier miles or hotel rewards.

Importance in B-to-B Markets: In business-to-business (B-to-B) contexts, relationships are highly dependent. A key strategy is “systems selling” or “solutions selling,” where a company provides a complete solution to a customer’s problem. Digital platforms like LinkedIn are also crucial for B-to-B engagement.

The Ultimate Goal: The fundamental purpose of building these customer and partner relationships is to consistently deliver superior value and satisfaction, which are the essential building blocks for customer loyalty and long-term profitability.



9.2.1.2.2 How Loyalty Programs Drive Customer Relationship Management (CRM)

CRM Foundation: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is the overall process of building and maintaining profitable customer relationships by delivering superior value and satisfaction. The core building blocks are customer value and satisfaction, which lead to loyalty and repeat business.

Loyalty Programs as a Key Tool: Loyalty programs, also known as “frequency marketing programs,” are a specific CRM tactic designed to reward customers who buy frequently or in large amounts, thereby strengthening their bond with the brand.

Hilton HHonors as a Prime Example (p.39): The Hilton HHonors program demonstrates an effective loyalty strategy by combining traditional rewards (points for free stays and upgrades) with modern technology. Its smartphone app significantly enhances the customer experience with features like digital check-in, room selection from a floor plan, and a digital key, making the customer’s stay more convenient and personal.

The Ultimate Goal: Customer Equity: By delivering enhanced value and a personalized experience, programs like Hilton HHonors aim to increase customer loyalty and a company’s “share of customer.” This directly contributes to building customer equity, which is the total combined lifetime value of all a company’s current and potential customers, serving as a key indicator of the firm’s future success.



9.2.1.2.3 Transforming CRM with Digital and Social Marketing
A New Era of Customer Engagement
Digital marketing has fundamentally changed Customer Relationship Management (CRM) by creating a new environment for customer interactions.

The Core Shift: The model has moved from “marketing by intrusion” (interrupting consumers) to “marketing by attraction” (creating engaging content that pulls consumers in).

Customer-Engagement Marketing: The primary goal is to foster direct and continuous customer involvement in shaping brand conversations, experiences, and communities, making the brand a meaningful part of consumers’ lives.

Customer-Managed Relationships: Empowered by digital tools and vast information, consumers now have more control, actively connecting with companies and each other to shape their own brand experiences.

Brand Advocacy: The aim is to create satisfied customers who will initiate positive word-of-mouth and favorable interactions about a brand on their own.




9.2.1.3 Key Digital Marketing Tools and Strategies
Companies use a diverse and evolving set of digital tools to build and manage customer relationships.

Online Presence: This includes foundational assets like brand websites for promotion, online brand community sites where customers can connect (e.g., Fitbit’s Community, Sephora’s Beauty Insider), and branded blogs and mobile apps for deeper engagement.

Social Media Platforms: Brands maintain active presences on platforms like Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, and TikTok to start conversations, address service issues, and research customer reactions.

Mobile Marketing: Due to the constant presence of smartphones, mobile marketing is crucial. It is highly personal, targeted, and always-on.

Customer-Generated Marketing: This strategy involves customers in content creation, either by invitation or by using their uninvited brand-related posts.

Influencer Marketing: This involves partnering with influencers to spread brand messages.

Brand-Created Programs: Brands also create their own advocates, such as the “Disney Social Media Moms.”



9.2.1.4 Leveraging Data, Analytics, and AI
The digital landscape generates “big data,” which companies analyze to enhance their CRM efforts.

Gaining Customer Insights: Marketing analytics and AI are used to analyze customer data from posts, transactions, and site visits to find meaningful patterns and improve products (e.g., Fitbit analyzing posts to fix product issues).

Personalization: AI enables the personalization of marketing offers and customer experiences at scale (e.g., Starbucks’ “Deep Brew” AI and Sephora’s Color IQ technology).

Real-time Engagement: Social media command centers (used by companies like Mastercard and IKEA) monitor online conversations in real-time to respond to feedback and manage service issues.

Advanced Targeting: Marketers use behavioral targeting (based on web browsing history) and social targeting (based on social media activity and connections) to deliver highly relevant ads.



9.2.1.5 Critical Challenges and Considerations
Despite its power, this approach comes with significant responsibilities and challenges.

Consumer Privacy and Data Security: The vast collection of personal data raises serious privacy concerns, requiring companies to be transparent and comply with regulations like GDPR and CCPA to maintain trust.

Authenticity and Trust: Customer-generated and influencer campaigns must be perceived as authentic, as they can easily backfire if they seem exploitative or disingenuous.

Information Overload: Companies can be overwhelmed by “big data” and need sophisticated analytics to turn raw data into actionable insights.

Integrated Strategy: The most successful approach is to blend new digital methods with traditional marketing to create a smoothly integrated and cohesive overall strategy.



9.2.1.6 The Role of Customer-Generated Marketing in CRM
Customer-generated marketing refers to brand content and experiences created by customers themselves, both by invitation and organically. It’s a core component of modern Customer Relationship Management (CRM) and customer-engagement marketing, reflecting a shift towards “customer-managed relationships” where empowered consumers actively shape brand conversations.


9.2.1.7 The Role of Partner Relationship Management in CRM

The Core Principle: Partner Relationship Management expands on CRM by recognizing that a company cannot create superior customer value on its own. It is the process of collaborating closely with partners, both inside and outside the company, to jointly build and deliver value to the end customer.

Internal Partners: This involves treating every department within the company, such as design, production, finance, and marketing, as a partner in an internal value chain. Success depends on all departments coordinating their activities and maintaining a shared, customer-focused mindset to ensure a smooth process from product creation to delivery.

External Partners: Companies must also build a strong value delivery network with external entities. This includes treating suppliers as collaborative partners to ensure a dependable supply of quality materials and working closely with marketing intermediaries (like distributors and retailers) by providing them with support to help them sell more effectively.

Application in B-to-B Markets: Partnerships are especially critical in business-to-business (B-to-B) relationships, which often involve high levels of interdependence. A key strategy is “solutions selling,” which involves providing a complete packaged solution to a customer’s problem rather than just a single product. Digital and social media are also essential tools for engaging business customers and managing these complex partnerships.



9.2.1.8 Customer Lifetime Value

9.2.1.8.1 What is Customer Lifetime Value?
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) represents the entire stream of purchases a customer will make over a lifetime of patronage. This concept is vital because it shifts focus from the profit of a single sale to the immense value of a long-term relationship. A company might even lose money on an initial transaction if it leads to a loyal, high-value customer who makes repeat purchases for years. The ultimate goal is to create customer delight, fostering an emotional bond that ensures they keep coming back.


9.2.1.8.2 CLV’s Link to Loyalty and Customer Equity
Good CRM is the engine for high CLV because it builds customer satisfaction and loyalty. Keeping customers loyal makes strong economic sense, as it’s estimated to be five times cheaper to retain an existing customer than to acquire a new one. Losing a customer means forfeiting their entire future spending potential, which can represent a massive financial loss.
CLV is also the fundamental building block of customer equity, the total combined CLVs of all a company’s current and potential customers. Many consider customer equity a better measure of a firm’s future performance than current sales or market share because it’s a forward-looking metric. The ultimate purpose of a strong CRM strategy is to produce high customer equity.


9.2.1.8.3 Strategies for Maximizing CLV
Companies use CRM not just to manage relationships but to actively increase their value. This involves treating customers as assets and managing them differently based on their potential.

Customer Segmentation: A key strategy is to classify customers based on their potential profitability and projected loyalty, then manage each group accordingly. The four main groups are:

Strangers: Low profit and low loyalty. The goal is to not invest in them.

Butterflies: Profitable but not loyal. The goal is to maximize profit in the short time they are customers.

True Friends: Both profitable and loyal. These are the most valuable customers, and the goal is to make continuous investments to retain and grow them.

Barnacles: Highly loyal but not very profitable. The company should try to increase their profitability or, if unsuccessful, end the relationship.


Increasing “Share of Customer”: Beyond retention, companies aim to capture a larger portion of a customer’s spending in their product categories. This is typically achieved through targeted cross-selling and up-selling programs.

Data and Analytics: Modern CRM relies heavily on “big data” and analytics to get a 360-degree view of the customer. By analyzing purchase history, website visits, and service calls, companies can gain deep insights, personalize experiences, and pinpoint high-value customers, all of which help to maximize loyalty and CLV.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.3 Company-Wide Strategic Planning
Strategic planning is crucial for long-run survival and growth, adapting the firm to opportunities in a changing environment. It involves:

Defining the Company Mission: A statement of the organization’s purpose, which should be market-oriented, specific, motivating, and focus on customer and societal benefits. For example, Starbucks sells “the ‘Starbucks Experience,’ one that enriches people’s lives one moment, one human being, one extraordinary cup of coffee at a time.”



Setting Company Objectives and Goals: Translating the mission into detailed supporting objectives for all management levels.

Designing the Business Portfolio: The collection of businesses and products that make up the company, aiming for the best fit between environmental opportunities and company strengths/weaknesses. The BCG Growth-Share Matrix classifies Strategic Business Units (SBUs) into Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs based on market growth rate and relative market share to guide investment decisions.

Developing Functional Plans: Each business unit and product team creates detailed plans to support the company’s strategic plan, including marketing.


9.3.1 The BCG Growth-Share Matrix for Strategic Planning
The Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Growth-Share Matrix is a classic portfolio-planning tool that helps companies analyze their collection of businesses or products, known as Strategic Business Units (SBUs). Its goal is to help management decide which SBUs to invest in, maintain, harvest, or divest based on their market position.

9.3.1.1 How the Matrix Works
The matrix classifies all of a company’s SBUs based on two key dimensions to provide a snapshot of the business portfolio’s health and potential.

Market Growth Rate: This measures the attractiveness and growth potential of the market the SBU operates in.

Relative Market Share: This measures the company’s strength and market leadership within that market compared to its largest competitor.



9.3.1.2 The Four SBU Quadrants
Based on their position on the grid, SBUs fall into one of four categories, each with a different strategic implication:

Stars: High-growth, high-share businesses. They are market leaders that need heavy investment to fuel their rapid growth and are expected to become future cash cows.

Cash Cows: Low-growth, high-share businesses. They are established and successful, generating more cash than they need. This cash can be used to support other SBUs.

Question Marks: High-growth, low-share businesses. Their future is uncertain. They require significant cash to grow market share, and management must decide whether to invest heavily to turn them into stars or phase them out.

Dogs: Low-growth, low-share businesses. They may generate enough cash to sustain themselves but are not significant profit sources and can drain resources and management attention.



9.3.1.3 Strategic Actions and Limitations
Once SBUs are classified, a company can choose one of four strategies:

Build: Invest more to increase the SBU’s share, primarily applicable to promising question marks and stars.

Hold: Invest just enough to maintain the SBU’s share at the current level, suitable for some stars and cash cows.

Harvest: Milk the SBU for its short-term cash flow, even if it negatively impacts long-term prospects. This is typically applied to shrinking cash cows, less promising question marks, and some dogs.

Divest: Sell off or phase out the SBU to reallocate resources elsewhere, generally for unpromising question marks and dogs.

However, the BCG matrix has significant limitations. It can be difficult and costly to implement, and its rigid, centralized structure struggles in today’s fast-moving or disruptive markets. Because of this, many companies now prefer more customized, decentralized planning methods that empower teams to make quicker, data-driven decisions.



9.3.2 Growth Strategies
Companies grow through various strategies:

Market Penetration: Increasing sales of current products to current market segments without changing the product (e.g., adding more stores, improving advertising).

Market Development: Identifying and developing new market segments for current company products (e.g., new demographic groups or geographic markets).

Product Development: Offering modified or new products to current markets (e.g., Starbucks developing instant coffee or plant-based milk options).

Diversification: Starting up or acquiring businesses outside the company’s current products and markets.



9.3.3 Crafting a Customer Value-Driven Marketing Strategy

9.3.3.1 Step 1: Segmentation and Targeting (Who to Serve)
First, a company decides which customers it’s best equipped to serve. This involves a two-part process:

Market Segmentation: This is the process of dividing a broad market into smaller, distinct groups of buyers who have different needs, characteristics, or behaviors.

Market Targeting: After defining segments, the company evaluates each one and selects the most promising segments to enter, those where it can generate the greatest customer value profitably.



9.3.3.2 Step 2: Differentiation and Positioning (How to Serve)
Next, the company decides on its value proposition, how it will create unique value for its chosen customers.

Differentiation: This involves creating a market offering that is genuinely superior to the competition by providing unique or different benefits.

Positioning: This is the act of arranging for the product to occupy a clear, distinctive, and desirable place in the minds of target consumers relative to competing products. A strong positioning is always built on a clear differentiation.



9.3.3.3 Step 3: The Integrated Marketing Mix (The Four Ps)
With the strategy defined, the company develops its marketing mix, the set of tactical tools it blends to get the desired response from the target market. These are known as the Four Ps:

Product: The need-satisfying offering itself (goods, services, experiences).

Price: The amount customers must pay.

Place: How the offering is made available to customers.

Promotion: Activities that communicate the offering’s value and persuade customers to buy it.

A modern perspective suggests also considering the buyer’s view, known as the Four As (Acceptability, Affordability, Accessibility, and Awareness), as a foundation for the Four Ps.


9.3.3.4 Managing and Measuring the Marketing Effort
Finally, a good strategy requires excellent execution and management.

Marketing Implementation is the process of turning strategic plans into concrete marketing actions. [Contents of a Marketing Plan template, p. 78]

Marketing Control involves measuring the results of these actions against the objectives and making necessary adjustments.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.4 The Marketing Environment
Companies must continually monitor and adapt to the marketing environment, which consists of:

Microenvironment: Actors close to the company affecting its ability to serve customers, including:

The Company: All departments must work together in an “internal value chain” to create customer value.

Suppliers: Provide resources and are often treated as partners

Marketing Intermediaries: Help promote, sell, and distribute products

Competitors: Companies must gain strategic advantage by positioning offerings strongly.

Publics: Any group with an actual or potential interest in or impact on an organization (financial, media, government, citizen-action, local, general, internal).

Customers: The most important actors; target markets can include consumer, business, reseller, government, and international markets.


Macroenvironment: Larger societal forces that affect the microenvironment, including:

Demographic Environment: Studies human populations. Key trends include changing age structures (Baby Boomers, Gen X, Millennials, Gen Z, Gen Alpha), shifting family structures, geographic population shifts, a better-educated population, and increasing diversity.

Economic Environment: Factors affecting consumer purchasing power and spending patterns. Consumers in the post-recession and pandemic eras often show “an enthusiasm for frugality rather than reverting to their old free-spending ways.”

Natural Environment: Physical environment and natural resources. Growing concern for “environmental sustainability” means meeting present needs without compromising future generations.

Technological Environment: Forces creating new technologies, leading to new product and market opportunities. It is “perhaps the most dramatic force reshaping our world.”

Political Environment: Laws, government agencies, and pressure groups influencing organizations and individuals.

Cultural Environment: Institutions and forces affecting society’s basic values, perceptions, preferences, and behaviors.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.5 Managing Marketing Information and Customer Insights
text:
9.5 Managing Marketing Information and Customer Insights
Effective marketing relies on “deep insights into what customers need and want.”

Big Data

Customer Insight Teams

Marketing Information Ecosystem (MIE): A system of people, processes, and assets dedicated to assessing information needs, developing information, and helping managers apply it to generate actionable insights. This involves:

Assessing Information Needs

Developing Needed Information: Sourcing from:

Internal Databases

Competitive Marketing Intelligence

Marketing Research

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.6 Consumer Buying Behavior
Consumer buyer behavior refers to the buying behavior of individuals and households for personal consumption. It is influenced by four major factors:

Cultural Factors:

Culture: The most basic determinant of wants and behavior

Subculture: Groups with shared value systems

Social Class: Relatively permanent and ordered divisions whose members share similar values, interests, and behaviors.


Social Factors:

Groups:

Membership Groups: Direct influence (e.g., family, friends).

Reference Groups: Serve as comparison points


Online Social Networks: Digital communities for socializing and exchanging information.

Family: The most important consumer buying unit.

Roles and Status: A person’s position in groups.


Personal Factors:

Age and Life-Cycle Stage: Changes in needs and wants over a lifetime

Occupation: Affects goods and services purchased.

Economic Situation: Trends in spending, income, savings, interest rates affect buying.

Lifestyle: A person’s pattern of living, expressed through activities, interests, and opinions.

Personality and Self-Concept: Unique psychological characteristics. Brand personality (sincerity, excitement, competence, sophistication, ruggedness) attracts consumers whose personalities match.


Psychological Factors:

Motivation: Needs become a motive (drive) when strong enough. Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualization) explains why people are driven by particular needs.

Perception: How people select, organize, and interpret information. Selective attention, selective distortion, and selective retention mean marketers must work hard to get messages through.

Learning: Changes in behavior from experience. Marketers use strong drives, motivating cues, and positive reinforcement.

Beliefs and Attitudes: Descriptive thoughts and consistently favorable or unfavorable evaluations, feelings, and tendencies. Attitudes are difficult to change, so companies often try to fit products into existing attitude patterns.



9.6.1 The Buyer Decision Process
Consists of five stages, marketers must focus on the entire process:

Need Recognition: Triggered by internal (hunger, thirst) or external stimuli (advertisements). Marketers aim to be on the consumer’s radar early.

Information Search: Consumers seek information from personal (friends, family), commercial (ads, websites), public (media, reviews), and experiential sources. Personal sources are often most effective for legitimizing or evaluating products. User-generated content and online reviews are increasingly important.

Evaluation of Alternatives: Consumers use information to evaluate different brands in their choice set.

Purchase Decision: The buyer’s decision on which brand to purchase. Influenced by attitudes of others and unexpected situational factors.

Postpurchase Behavior: Satisfaction or dissatisfaction based on the relationship between expectations and perceived performance. Customer satisfaction is key to loyalty and word-of-mouth. Cognitive dissonance (buyer discomfort from postpurchase conflict) can occur.



9.6.2 Types of Buying Decision Behavior

Complex Buying Behavior: High involvement, significant perceived brand differences (e.g., buying a car).

Dissonance-Reducing Buying Behavior: High involvement, few perceived brand differences (e.g., expensive carpet).

Habitual Buying Behavior: Low involvement, few perceived brand differences (e.g., salt).

Variety-Seeking Buying Behavior: Low involvement, significant perceived brand differences (e.g., cookies).



9.6.3 New Product Adoption and Diffusion Process
New Product: Perceived as new by potential customers.
Adoption Process: Individual’s journey from first learning to final adoption (regular use). Stages: Awareness, Interest, Evaluation, Trial, Adoption.
Adopter Categories: Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Mainstream, Late Mainstream, Lagging Adopters. Marketers often target innovators and early adopters.
Product Characteristics Influencing Adoption: Relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, observability, and risk.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.7 Business Buyer Behavior
Business buyer behavior involves organizations buying goods and services for use in production, for resale, or for use in general operations.

9.7.1 Characteristics of Business Markets

Market Structure and Demand: Fewer but larger buyers, geographically concentrated. Demand is derived demand (ultimately from consumer goods), often inelastic and more fluctuating.

Nature of the Buying Unit: Involves more decision participants and a more professional purchasing effort, often by trained purchasing agents.

Types of Decisions and the Decision Process: Business purchases are more complex, involve more formal procedures, and often lead to stronger buyer-seller dependency and partnerships (e.g., Boeing working closely with airlines).



9.7.2 Types of Business Buying Situations
Straight Rebuy: Routine reorder without modification.
Modified Rebuy: Buyer seeks to modify product specifications, prices, terms, or suppliers.
New Task: Company buying a product or service for the first time. This is the marketer’s greatest opportunity.
Systems Selling (Solutions Selling): Buying a complete solution to a problem from a single seller rather than separate products and services (e.g., ChemStation’s industrial cleaning solutions).


9.7.3 Major Influences on Business Buyers

Environmental: Economic outlook, supply of key materials, technological, political, and competitive developments, culture, and customs. Major disruptions like COVID-19 have accelerated the shift to digital B-to-B interactions.

Organizational: Objectives, strategies, structure, systems, and procedures of the buying organization.

Interpersonal: Influence among participants in the buying center.

Individual: Personal motives, preferences, and buying styles of participants.



9.7.4 The Business Buyer Decision Process
Eight stages for a new task buying situation:

Problem Recognition: Internal or external stimuli recognize a need.

General Need Description: Describe general characteristics and quantity of needed item.

Product Specification: Develop technical specifications, often with value analysis.

Supplier Search: Find the best vendors (e.g., online searches, trade directories).

Proposal Solicitation: Invite qualified suppliers to submit proposals.

Supplier Selection: Review proposals and select suppliers. Many prefer multiple sources for dependency management.

Order-Routine Specification: Final order, including technical specifications, quantity, delivery, return policies, warranties. May involve vendor-managed inventory.

Performance Review: Assess supplier performance, which can lead to continuation, modification, or termination of the arrangement.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.8 Customer Value–Driven Marketing Strategy
This briefing document synthesizes key concepts across customer value-driven marketing strategy, product and service management, pricing, distribution, and promotion, with a significant emphasis on the transformative impact of digital technologies. Modern marketing is no longer about mass approaches but about target marketing, understanding individual customer needs, and building deep, lasting relationships. Companies must systematically engage customers by creating superior value at every touchpoint, from initial segmentation and product design to dynamic pricing, integrated channel strategies, and compelling digital communications. The digital environment presents both immense opportunities and complex challenges, including evolving consumer behavior, ethical considerations around data privacy, and the need for agile, data-driven decision-making.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.9 Customer Value-Driven Marketing Strategy (STDP)
The foundation of modern marketing lies in designing customer value-driven strategies, a four-step process: Segmentation, Targeting, Differentiation, and Positioning (STDP). The core goal is to “create more value for the customers we serve than competitors do.”

9.9.1 Market Segmentation:

Definition: Dividing a market into distinct groups of buyers with different needs, characteristics, or behaviors who might require separate marketing strategies or mixes.

Bases for Segmentation:

Geographic: Dividing markets by nations, regions, cities, or even neighborhoods. Companies increasingly localize offerings to fit specific geographic needs. (e.g., McDonald’s adapting menus globally).

Demographic: Dividing markets by age, life-cycle stage, gender, income, occupation, education, religion, ethnicity, and generation.

Psychographic: Dividing markets by social class, lifestyle, or personality characteristics.


Behavioral: Dividing markets based on consumer knowledge, attitudes, uses of a product, or responses to a product.

Occasion Segmentation: Grouping buyers by when they get the idea to buy, make a purchase, or use an item.

Benefit Segmentation: Grouping buyers according to the different benefits they seek from a product.

Requirements for Effective Segmentation: Segments must be Measurable, Accessible, Substantial, Differentiable, and Actionable.



9.9.2 Market Targeting:

Definition: Evaluating each market segment’s attractiveness and selecting one or more segments to serve.

Targeting Strategies:

Undifferentiated (Mass) Marketing: Targeting the whole market with one offer. Less common today.

Differentiated (Segmented) Marketing: Targeting several market segments and designing separate offers for each. Risks include increased costs and potential cannibalization.

Concentrated (Niche) Marketing: Going after a larger share of one or a few smaller segments or niches. Effective for limited resources.


Micromarketing: Tailoring products and marketing programs to suit the tastes of specific individuals and local customer segments.

Local Marketing: Tailoring brands and promotions to local customer needs and wants.

Individual Marketing (One-to-One/Mass Customization): Tailoring products and marketing programs to individual customers.

Socially Responsible Targeting: “Controversies arise when marketers attempt to profit at the expense of targeted segments—when they unfairly target vulnerable segments or target them with questionable products or tactics.” Ethical targeting serves both company and targeted segment interests.



9.9.3 Differentiation:

Definition: Differentiating the market offering to create superior customer value.

Dimensions of Differentiation:

Product: Features, performance, style, and design.

Services: Offering superior or faster delivery and installation.

Channels: Gaining competitive advantage through channel design, coverage, expertise, and performance.

People: Hiring and training better people than competitors.

Image: Conveying a product’s distinctive benefits and positioning through brand image and symbols.


Criteria for Valuable Differentiation: A difference should be Important, Distinctive, Superior, Communicable, Preemptive, Affordable, and Profitable.



9.9.4 Positioning:

Definition: “The way a product is defined by consumers on important attributes—the place the product occupies in consumers’ minds relative to competing products. Products are made in factories, but brands happen in the minds of consumers.”

Value Propositions (Overall Positioning Strategies):

More for More: Most upscale product/service with higher price. (e.g., Four Seasons hotels, Rolex watches).

More for the Same: High quality at the same price as competitors.

The Same for Less: Good quality at a lower price. (e.g., ALDI store brands).

Less for Much Less: Basic quality at a very low price.

More for Less: Best product at the lowest price (difficult to sustain).


Positioning Statement: Summarizes positioning using the form: “For (target customers), who (unsolved customer needs), our product is (product description), that provides (key benefits). Unlike (key competing brands), our product (points of difference).” (e.g., Public Goods positioned as providing “healthy, sustainable, everyday essentials at a fair price”).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.10 Product and Service Management
Products are more than just tangible items; they are complex bundles of benefits designed to satisfy customer needs.

9.10.1 Product Levels:

Core Customer Value: The fundamental problem-solving benefits consumers seek.

Actual Product: Includes quality level, features, design, brand name, and packaging.

Augmented Product: Additional services and benefits, such as warranty, installation, after-sale support.



9.10.2 Product Classifications:

Product and Service Classifications P.252

Consumer Products: Bought by final consumers for personal consumption (convenience, shopping, specialty, unsought).

Industrial Products: Bought by individuals and organizations for further processing or business use.

Other Marketable Entities: Organizations (corporate marketing), Persons (person marketing, e.g., Serena Williams), Places (place marketing, e.g., tourism), and Ideas (social marketing, e.g., Microsoft’s “Make What’s Next” campaign).



9.10.3 Individual Product Decisions:

Product Attributes: Quality (performance quality and consistency), features (competitive differentiation), style, and design.

Branding: A “name, term, sign, symbol, or design…that identifies the maker or seller of a product or service.” Branding adds value and differentiates. (e.g., Payless’s “Palessi” stunt illustrating brand power, Avocados From Mexico creating a visible brand in a commodity category).

Packaging: Designing the container or wrapper. Emerged as a driver of customer satisfaction and competitive advantage, performing sales tasks. (e.g., P&G’s Child-Guard Pack for Tide PODS, smart packaging with QR codes).

Labeling and Logos: Identify, describe, and promote the brand. Logos are evolving for digital media. (e.g., IHOP and Audi logo redesigns).

Product Support Services: Enhancing customer satisfaction and protecting against competitors.



9.10.4 Product Line Decisions:

Product Line: A group of closely related products.

Line Stretching: Lengthening a product line beyond its current range.

Line Filling: Adding more items within the present range of the line.



9.10.5 Product Mix Decisions:

Product Mix (Portfolio): All product lines and items a seller offers. (e.g., Colgate-Palmolive’s oral care, personal care, home care, and pet care lines).

Dimensions:

Width: Number of different product lines.

Length: Total number of items within product lines.

Depth: Number of versions offered for each product in the line.


Consistency: How closely related the various product lines are.

Companies can increase business by adding new lines (width), lengthening existing lines, deepening product mixes, or pursuing more/less consistency. (e.g., Ford and GM streamlining product mixes to focus on SUVs/trucks and electric vehicles).


9.10.6 Services Marketing:

Four Characteristics:

Intangibility: Services “cannot be seen, tasted, felt, heard, or smelled before they are bought.” Marketers must make the service tangible and send the right quality signals (e.g., Oberoi Hotels’ integrated tangible elements).

Inseparability: Services are produced and consumed simultaneously and cannot be separated from their providers.

Variability: Quality depends on who provides them, when, where, and how.

Perishability: Services cannot be stored for later sale or use.


Service Profit Chain: Links “service customer satisfaction and firm profits with employee satisfaction.” Includes internal service quality, satisfied/productive employees, greater service value, and satisfied/loyal customers.

Three Marketing Tasks for Service Companies:

Managing Service Differentiation: Developing a differentiated offering, delivery, and image. (e.g., CVS Minute Clinics).

Managing Service Quality: Setting high standards and striving for “close-to-perfect service performance.” Good service recovery can turn angry customers into loyal ones. (e.g., Southwest Airlines’ rapid social media response).

Managing Service Productivity: Training employees, industrializing service, and using technology. Companies “must be careful not to take service out of the service.”





9.10.7 Branding Strategy: Building Strong Brands:

Brand Equity: “The differential effect that knowing the brand name has on a customer’s emotions, attitudes, and behaviors related to the product and its marketing.” A measure of preference and loyalty. Strong brands increase willingness to pay and competitive leverage.

Brand Positioning: Beyond attributes and benefits, the strongest brands are “positioned on strong beliefs, values, and feelings, thereby engaging customers on a deep, emotional level.” Brands need both a “cold side” (rational) and a “hot side” (emotional). (e.g., Rolex combining luxury features with feelings of status and success).

Brand Name Selection: Should suggest benefits, be easy to pronounce/remember/recognize, be distinctive, and be extendable. Must be legally protected (e.g., Kleenex fighting genericide).

Brand Sponsorship:

National Brands (Manufacturer’s Brands): (e.g., Samsung, Kellogg).

Store Brands (Private Labels): Retailer-owned brands. Offer greater selection and quality, often out-innovating national brands.

Licensed Brands: Using names/symbols created by other companies/individuals for a fee.


Co-Branding: “Two established brand names of different companies are used on the same product.” Creates broader appeal and greater equity. (e.g., Uber/Spotify, Taco Bell/Doritos).

Brand Development Strategies:

Line Extensions: Extending existing brand names to new forms, colors, sizes, ingredients, or flavors within an existing product category. Low-cost, low-risk. Risks confusion or cannibalization. (e.g., Doritos’ numerous flavors).

Brand Extensions: Extending a current brand name to new or modified products in a new category. Creates familiarity and acceptance at lower costs. Risks confusing the main brand image or failing in the new category. (e.g., Dyson extending to lighting).

Multibrands: Marketing many different brands in a given product category. Appeals to different segments, locks up shelf space, captures larger market share. (e.g., PepsiCo’s numerous beverage brands). Risks spreading resources too thin.


New Brands: Creating new brand names when entering a new product category for which existing brands are inappropriate.

Managing Brands: Continuous communication of positioning, not just through advertising but through “customers’ engagement with brands and customers’ brand experiences.” (e.g., Disney’s “thousand small gestures”). Periodic brand audits.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.11 New Product Development and Product Life Cycle Strategies
text:
9.11 New Product Development and Product Life Cycle Strategies
Companies that excel at developing and managing new products reap big rewards. Every product has a life cycle.

9.11.1 New Product Development Process (8 Steps):

Idea Generation: Systematic search for new product ideas from internal and external sources (customers, competitors, distributors, suppliers, innovation leaders). (e.g., Facebook’s hackathons, 3M’s Post-it Notes from unexpected discovery, Clorox repurposing existing products, Craftsman unbundling offerings).

Idea Screening: Reducing the number of ideas based on criteria.

Concept Development & Testing

Concept Development: Developing a detailed version of the new product idea in meaningful consumer terms. (e.g., HondaJet considering different target markets for its jets).

Concept Testing: Testing new product concepts with target consumers (e.g., survey questions for HondaJet FamilyV).


Marketing Strategy Development: Designing an initial marketing strategy for the new product (target market, value proposition, price, distribution, marketing budget).

Business Analysis: Reviewing sales, costs, and profit projections.

Product Development: Developing the concept into a physical product or detailed service blueprint. Involves rigorous testing and customer input (e.g., Carhartt Crew community). Patent and trademark protection.

Test Marketing: Testing the product and proposed marketing program in realistic market settings. Helps refine the product and program before full launch. High costs and time can be drawbacks. (e.g., McDonald’s extensive test marketing for McPlant, Workzone’s A/B testing for website design).

Commercialization: Introducing the new product into the market. High costs for manufacturing, advertising, and promotion. (e.g., Sony’s PS5 launch, Microsoft’s Surface tablet launch).



Customer-Centered New Product Development: Begins and ends with understanding and involving customers. (e.g., ASICS using Shoe Finder for personalized recommendations).

Team-Based New Product Development: Various company departments working closely together, overlapping steps to save time and increase effectiveness.

Systematic New Product Development: Holistic and systematic, using an innovation management system to collect, review, evaluate, and manage ideas. (e.g., Apple’s “deep expertise, a laser focus on details, and a willingness to collaborate”).



9.11.2 Product Life Cycle (PLC):
The course of a product’s sales and profits over its lifetime (Product Development, Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline).

Product Development: Begins when company finds and develops an idea. Sales are zero, investment costs are high.

Introduction: New product launched. Slow sales growth, negative or low profits due to high expenses. Focus on informing consumers and attracting distributors.

Growth: Rapid sales growth and increasing profits. Strategies include improving quality, adding features/models, entering new segments/channels, shifting advertising, lowering prices.

Maturity: Sales growth slows or levels off, profits stabilize. Companies may modify the market, product, or marketing mix. (e.g., Campbell’s encouraging usage with recipes, Samsung adding smart features to appliances).

Decline: Sales and profits dwindle. Companies may maintain, harvest (reduce costs, milk sales), or drop the product. (e.g., Hallmark reimagining greeting cards, Marvel defying decline by focusing on movies).

Fads: High initial sales, quick fizzle (e.g., fidget spinners).

Defying the PLC: Companies can reinvent products to stay in growth (e.g., Intuit’s “creative destruction” with QuickBooks).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.12 Pricing Strategies
Price is the only marketing mix element that produces revenue and plays a key role in signaling customer value.

9.12.1 Major Pricing Strategies:

Customer Value-Based Pricing: Setting prices based on buyers’ perceptions of value rather than on the seller’s costs. “Good value” is not always “low price.”

Good-Value Pricing: Offering the right combination of quality and good service at a fair price. (e.g., Kroger’s low-priced lines, Google Nest’s lower-priced thermostat).

Value-Added Pricing: Attaching value-added features and services to differentiate and support higher prices.


Cost-Based Pricing: Setting prices based on the costs of producing, distributing, and selling the product, plus a fair rate of return.

Types of Costs:

Fixed (overhead, do not vary with production)

Variable (vary directly with production). Total costs are the sum.



Experience Curve (Learning Curve): As production experience increases, average cost per unit falls. Risks include cheap image and competitors finding lower-cost technologies.

Cost-Plus Pricing (Markup Pricing): Adding a standard markup to the cost of the product. Simple but ignores demand and competition.

Break-Even Pricing (Target Return Pricing): Setting price to break even on costs or make a target return.

Competition-Based Pricing: Setting prices based on competitors’ strategies, costs, prices, and market offerings. The goal is “not to match or beat competitors’ prices. Rather, the goal is to set prices according to relative value.”



9.12.2 Other Pricing Considerations:

The Market and Demand: Understanding price elasticity.

The Economy: Economic conditions (recession, inflation) affect consumer spending and value perceptions. Marketers emphasize value-for-money. “Even in tough economic times, consumers do not buy based on prices alone.”

Reseller Reactions: How channel partners will react to prices.

Government and Social Concerns: Legal and ethical issues



9.12.3 New Product Pricing Strategies:

Market-Skimming Pricing (Price Skimming): Setting a high initial price to skim maximum revenues layer by layer from segments willing to pay the most, then lowering prices over time. (e.g., Apple’s new product launches).

Market-Penetration Pricing: Setting a low initial price to penetrate the market quickly and deeply to win a large market share. Requires a price-sensitive market, decreasing costs with volume, and ability to deter competition.



9.12.4 Product Mix Pricing:

Product Line Pricing: Setting price steps between different products in a line.

Optional-Product Pricing: Pricing optional or accessory products sold with the main product.

Captive-Product Pricing: Pricing products that must be used with the main product. (e.g., printer cartridges for printers).

By-Product Pricing: Pricing low-value by-products to offset disposal costs or generate revenue. (e.g., chicken feet for export).

Product Bundle Pricing: Combining several products and offering the bundle at a reduced price. Mixed bundling (separate or bundle) often leads to higher profits.



9.12.5 Price Adjustment Strategies:

Discount and Allowance Pricing: Reducing prices for volume purchases, early payment, or promotional support.

Segmented Pricing: Selling a product/service at two or more prices not based on cost differences (customer-segment, product-form, location-based, time-based). Must be effective, legal, and reflect perceived value.

Psychological Pricing: Considering the psychology of price, not just economics. Consumers use price to judge quality. Reference prices play a role. (e.g., $X.99 endings, Keurig Custom Smart making other models seem affordable).

Promotional Pricing: Temporarily reducing prices to spur short-run sales (discounts, special-event pricing, loss leaders, cash rebates, low-interest financing, longer warranties, free maintenance). Risks eroding brand value and creating “deal-prone” customers. (e.g., Bed Bath & Beyond’s coupon dilemma).

Geographical Pricing: Adjusting prices for different customer locations (FOB-origin, uniform-delivered, zone, basing-point, freight-absorption).

Dynamic and Personalized Pricing: Adjusting prices continually to meet changing conditions and individual customer situations in real time. Enabled by digital technologies. (e.g., airlines, hotels, online retailers like Amazon). Risks customer confusion and resentment if not transparent.

International Pricing: Adjusting prices for different world markets, considering economic conditions, competition, laws, and infrastructure.



9.12.6 Initiating and Responding to Price Changes:

Price Cuts: May be due to excess capacity, falling demand, or desire to dominate. Can lead to perception of lower quality.

Price Increases: May be due to inflation or increased demand. Can lead to accusations of gouging.

Competitor Reactions: Most likely when few competitors, highly substitutable products, and informed buyers.

Responses to Competitor Price Changes: Sit tight, reduce price, raise perceived quality, improve quality and raise price, or launch a low-price “fighter brand” (e.g., Intel Celeron).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.13 Distribution Channels and Logistics
Companies rarely work alone; their success depends on a value delivery network of suppliers, distributors, and customers.

9.13.1 Supply Chains and Value Delivery Networks:

Supply Chain: Upstream (firms supplying materials) and downstream (marketing channels/distribution channels).

Value Delivery Network: “Company, suppliers, distributors, and, ultimately, customers who ‘partner’ with each other to improve the performance of the entire system.”



9.13.2 Nature and Importance of Marketing Channels:

Marketing Channel (Distribution Channel): “A set of interdependent organizations that help make a product or service available for use or consumption.”

Channel decisions affect all other marketing decisions (pricing, promotion, product development). Imaginative distribution systems can provide competitive advantage



9.13.3 Channel Behavior and Organization:
Channel members are interdependent but often act in their own short-run self-interest, leading to channel conflict.

Conventional Distribution Channel: Independent firms maximizing their own profits.

Vertical Marketing System (VMS): Producers, wholesalers, and retailers acting as a unified system (corporate, contractual, administered).

Corporate VMS: Single ownership. (e.g., EssilorLuxottica, Tesla selling directly).

Contractual VMS: Independent firms join through contracts. (e.g., Franchise organizations like Planet Fitness).

Administered VMS: Coordination through the size and power of one party. (e.g., Walmart’s power over suppliers).


Horizontal Marketing System: Two or more companies at one level join together to pursue new opportunities. (e.g., Amazon and Best Buy selling smarthome devices).

Multichannel Distribution Systems: A single firm uses two or more marketing channels to reach one or more customer segments. Benefits include expanded sales and market coverage but risks channel conflict.



9.13.4 Channel Design Decisions:
Begins with analyzing customer needs and setting channel objectives.

Identifying Major Alternatives: Types of intermediaries, number of intermediaries (intensive, exclusive, selective distribution).

Evaluating Alternatives: Economic, control, and adaptability criteria. Must take a “whole-channel view” to maximize total company profitability.

Designing International Distribution Channels: Adapting to unique distribution systems and infrastructures in each country (e.g., small independent retailers in India, Nestlé’s floating supermarket in Brazil).



9.13.5 Channel Management Decisions:

Selecting Channel Members: Evaluating years in business, other lines, location, growth record, cooperativeness, reputation.

Managing and Motivating Channel Members: Viewing intermediaries as “first-line customers and partners,” practicing partner relationship management. (e.g., Toyota’s relationships with suppliers).

Evaluating Channel Members: Regular performance evaluation against standards.

Public Policy and Distribution Decisions: Laws prevent exclusionary tactics (exclusive distribution, exclusive dealing, tying agreements, full-line forcing). Producers have rights to select/terminate dealers “for cause.”



9.13.6 Marketing Logistics and Supply Chain Management:

Marketing Logistics (Physical Distribution): “Planning, implementing, and controlling the physical flow of goods, services, and related information from points of origin to points of consumption to meet customer requirements at a profit.” Includes inbound logistics, outbound logistics, and reverse logistics. Involves the entirety of supply chain management.

Importance: Competitive advantage through better service or lower prices, especially “last mile delivery.” (e.g., Amazon’s same-day delivery, Kroger’s curbside pickup, Starship’s delivery bots).

Sustainable Supply Chains: Reducing environmental impact. “Sustainable channels are not only good for the world, they can also be good for a company’s bottom line.” (e.g., Levi’s Water<Less innovations, IKEA’s “People & Planet Positive” strategy).

Major Logistics Functions: Warehousing (storage, distribution centers), inventory management (just-in-time, RFID), transportation (truck, rail, water, pipeline, air, multimodal), and logistics information management (EDI, VMI). (e.g., Walmart’s “On-Time, In-Full” deliveries).

Integrated Logistics Management: Teamwork within the company and among channel organizations to maximize performance of the entire distribution network. (e.g., UPS acting as a 3PL provider for Kingsdown).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.14 Promotion Mix and Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC)
text:
9.14 Promotion Mix and Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC)
Beyond creating value, marketers must “clearly and persuasively communicate that value.”

9.14.1 The Promotion Mix (Marketing Communications Mix):
“The specific blend of advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, and public relations tools that the company uses to engage consumers, persuasively communicate customer value, and build customer relationships.”

Advertising: Any paid form of nonpersonal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor.

Sales Promotion: Short-term incentives to encourage purchase.

Personal Selling: Personal presentations by the sales force to engage customers and make sales.

Public Relations (PR): Building good relations with various publics through favorable publicity, corporate image, and handling unfavorable rumors/events.

The entire marketing mix (product, price, place, promotion) must be coordinated for greatest impact.


9.14.2 Advertising Decisions:

Objectives: Informative (new products, building primary demand), Persuasive (competition, building brand preference), Reminder (mature products, maintaining relationships). Advertising aims to move consumers through the buying process.

Budget: Affordable method, percentage-of-sales, competitive-parity, objective-and-task. (e.g., Amazon’s huge ad spend).

Message and Content Strategy:

Message Strategy: Identifying customer benefits as content appeals.

Creative Concept (“Big Idea”): Bringing message strategy to life distinctly.

Advertising Appeals: Meaningful, believable, distinctive.


Message Execution: Approach, style, tone, words, format.

Media Decisions:Determining Reach, Frequency, Impact, Engagement: Media that will “engage consumers rather than simply reach them.”

Choosing Media Types: Television, digital/social media, print, radio, outdoor, alternative media. Companies shifting to digital for targeting and engagement.

Selecting Media Vehicles: Specific media within each type.

Deciding on Media Timing: Scheduling over time (seasonality, continuity).


Evaluation: Communication and sales effects (A/B testing for digital).

Socially Responsible Advertising: Avoiding bait-and-switch, promoting social responsibility (e.g., Dove’s Campaign for Real Beauty, NFL’s inclusive marketing).

International Advertising: Standardize vs. adapt (think globally, act locally). Addressing DEI issues.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.15 Public Relations (PR):

Tools: News, special events, written materials, corporate identity materials, public service activities. (e.g., CVS Health’s “CVS Quits for Good” campaign).

Role and Impact: Can have strong impact at lower cost than advertising. “PR pros are an organization’s master storytellers.” Works with advertising in IMC to build engagement. (e.g., State Street Global Advisors’ “Fearless Girl” statue).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.16 Personal Selling and Sales Promotion
Personal selling is the interpersonal arm of the promotion mix, while sales promotion offers short-term incentives.

9.16.1 Personal Selling:

Definition: “Personal presentations by the firm’s sales force for the purpose of engaging customers, making sales, and building customer relationships.”

Modern salespeople are “well-educated and well-trained professionals who add value for customers and maintain long-term customer relationships.”

Salesperson Classifications: Order takers vs. order getters (creative selling, relationship building).

Coordinating Marketing and Sales: Essential for customer value creation. Actions include increasing communication, creating joint objectives, and appointing liaisons.



9.16.2 Sales Force Management:

Designing Sales Force Strategy and Structure:

Sales Force Structure: Territorial, product, customer, or complex. (e.g., Vorwerk’s Kobold vacuum cleaner team by territory, P&G by customer and territory).

Sales Force Size: Workload approach. Salespeople are a productive but expensive asset.

Outside vs. Inside Sales Forces: Outside (field calls), Inside (office-based, often supporting outside sales).


Recruiting and Selecting Salespeople: Identifying traits of successful salespeople, recruiting from various sources, and selecting the best applicants.

Training Salespeople: Familiarizing with company history, products, policies, and selling art.

Compensating Salespeople: Fixed amount, variable amount, expenses, fringe benefits. Tends to “motivate, direct, and control.”

Supervising Salespeople: Guiding and motivating. (e.g., sales quotas, positive incentives like meetings and contests).

Evaluating Salespeople: Formal evaluation based on objective (sales, calls, profits) and subjective (customer feedback, product knowledge) measures. Top measures include customer satisfaction and retention.



9.16.3 The Personal Selling Process (Transaction-Oriented):

Prospecting and Qualifying: Identifying qualified potential customers.

Preapproach: Learning about the organization and buyers.

Approach: Meeting the buyer for the first time.

Presentation and Demonstration: Telling the “value story.”

Handling Objections: Seeking out, clarifying, and turning objections into reasons for buying.

Closing: Asking for the order.

Follow-Up: Ensuring customer satisfaction and repeat business.

Personal Selling and Managing Customer Relationships: The process must be understood in the context of building long-term relationships (relationship marketing), not just closing a single sale. Salespeople must adapt to new buying processes where customers research independently online. Value selling (selling value, not price) is crucial.



9.16.4 Sales Promotion:

Reasons for Growth: Product managers face pressure for short-run sales, increased competition/less differentiation, declining advertising efficiency, and deal-oriented consumers.

Should “help to reinforce the product’s value proposition and build customer relationships.”

Objectives: Consumer (boost buying, engagement), Trade (getting reseller support), Business (generate leads, purchases, motivate sales force).

Major Sales Promotion Tools:

Consumer Promotions: Samples (e.g., Ben & Jerry’s Free Cone Day), coupons, price packs, premiums (e.g., McDonald’s Happy Meals), advertising specialties, point-of-purchase (POP) displays, contests/sweepstakes/games (e.g., Lyft’s parking ticket contest), event marketing/sponsorships (e.g., Red Bull’s vast event marketing). Virtual events have gained prominence.

Trade Promotions: Discounts, allowances, free goods, push money, specialty advertising items.

Business Promotions: Conventions/trade shows, sales contests.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.17 Digital Marketing: Online, Social Media, and Mobile
text:
9.17 Digital Marketing: Online, Social Media, and Mobile
Digital marketing is transforming the landscape, offering new ways to engage customers and create value.

Defining Digital Marketing: “The use of technology-intensive platforms such as the internet, mobile networks and devices, and social media to engage directly with carefully targeted individual consumers, consumer communities, and businesses.”

Rapid Growth: Surging share of marketing spending and sales, with mobile ad spending exploding.

Benefits to Customers: Convenient, easy, private, anywhere/anytime access, highly targeted, immediate, sense of brand engagement and community. (e.g., LEGO Ideas forum, Peloton community).

Benefits to Sellers: Efficient reach, important for new/small companies, lower costs, great flexibility, immediate announcements/offers, shortens consumer decision journey, real-time marketing.


9.17.1 Preparing for a Digital Marketing Campaign:

Understanding the Digital Consumer: Developing detailed digital consumer personas (e.g., Shawna Warrington for a coffee kitchen startup).

Understanding Consumer Omni-Channel Navigation Behavior: Consumers use multiple channels (stores, catalogs, online, mobile, social media) across their buying journey. Marketers need to understand which channels are used at each stage. (e.g., journey for buying an OLED HDTV).

Using Experimentation (A/B Testing): Testing approaches and tactics before committing resources. Digital environment is friendly to rapid experimentation.



9.17.2 Digital Channels and Media:

Online Marketing: Via the internet.

Marketing Websites: Designed to engage customers and drive direct purchase.

Brand Community Websites: Present brand content to engage and create customer-brand communities. (e.g., Peloton’s online community, Patagonia’s “The Cleanest Line” blog).

Online Advertising and Promotions:

Online Display Ads: Appear on digital forums, often programmatically bought.

Search-Related Ads (Contextual Advertising): Text/image ads atop/alongside search engine results (pay-per-click). Critical for online visibility.


Email Marketing: Important for information sharing, customer acquisition (B2B), and retention. Permission-based marketing is crucial.

Online Videos: Promote products, give brands a voice, create positive associations. Viral marketing (creating infectious content).

Blogs and Other Online Forums: Platforms for sharing thoughts and content, building communities. (e.g., Oh My Disney blog).



9.17.3 Social Media Marketing:
“Independent and commercial online social networks where people congregate to socialize and share messages, opinions, videos, pictures, and other content.”

Characteristics: Interactive, immediate, timely, cost-effective, support deep engagement and community. (e.g., JetBlue’s social media interactions).

Platforms: Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, YouTube, TikTok, LinkedIn, Pinterest, livestreaming platforms (e.g., Twitch).

Metaverse Platforms: Immersive digital environments for work, play, learning, socializing, shopping. “Still in its infancy but growing explosively.” (e.g., Meta’s Horizon Worlds, Roblox, Chipotle’s Roblox games).

Mobile Marketing: Marketing messages, promotions, and content delivered to on-the-go consumers via mobile devices.

Engaging Consumers: Goes beyond buying to creating ongoing brand experiences and community. (e.g., TripAdvisor’s travel app, adidas Runtastic apps).

Interactive Features: Many platforms add features for immersive mobile engagements. (e.g., Arby’s Escape Room game on Instagram, INFINITI’s Quick Reactions quiz stickers).

Challenge: Developing “valued mobile offers, ads, and apps that make customers want to come calling” in a cluttered environment.



9.17.4 Omni-Channel Marketing:
“Creating a seamless cross-channel buying experience that integrates in-store, digital, and mobile shopping.”

Customers are increasingly adept at omni-channel navigation.

Traditional retailers integrating online, online-only retailers opening physical stores. (e.g., Amazon opening physical stores, CarMax’s seamless digital/in-store experience, Home Depot’s “One Home Depot” strategy, Walmart’s omni-channel transformation).

Requires breaking down historical silos and creating fluid structures across channels (e.g., Oasis fashion retailer).

Even channels not highly profitable directly can contribute strongly to overall profits (e.g., catalogs driving online/in-store sales for L.L.Bean).



9.17.5 Public Policy Issues in Digital Marketing:

Consumer Privacy: Marketers knowing too much about consumers. Consumers concerned about data tracking and security. (e.g., Google’s data collection, Equifax breach).

Irritation, Deception, and Fraud: Spam, cluttered ads, tricksters, fraudsters.

Data Integrity and Security: Fears of identity theft and network infiltration.

Antitrust Concerns: Dominance of online behemoths (e.g., Apple’s app store, Amazon, Google).

Need for Action: Marketers acting to prevent abuses (e.g., Digital Advertising Alliance principles) and government regulations (e.g., do-not-call lists, CAN-SPAM, GDPR).

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
section: 9.18 Introduction: The Evolving Landscape of Marketing
text:
9.18 Introduction: The Evolving Landscape of Marketing
This briefing document synthesizes key themes and important concepts from the provided sources, focusing on competitive strategy, global marketing, and sustainable marketing. In today’s dynamic marketplace, companies must navigate fierce competition, rapidly changing technological environments, and increasing demands for social and environmental responsibility. Successful marketing extends beyond simply satisfying immediate customer needs to building long-term value, understanding competitors, and operating with a global and sustainable mindset.

title: 9  Principles of Marketing
text:
9.19 Creating Competitive Advantage
The core of successful business lies in understanding and outperforming competitors. This involves a multi-faceted approach to analyzing the competitive landscape and developing strategic positioning.

9.19.1 Competitor Analysis: Understanding the Battlefield
Companies must develop a deep understanding of their competitors, not just their customers. This analysis is a multi-step process:

9.19.1.1 Identifying Competitors:

Narrow View: Initially, competitors might seem to be firms offering similar products/services to the same customers at similar prices (e.g., Facebook seeing Twitter, TikTok as competitors).

Broad View (Competitor Myopia): Companies must avoid “competitor myopia,” which means not recognizing broader, latent competitors. Kodak, for instance, failed to see digital camera makers as its true rivals. Amazon, initially an online bookstore, expanded its view to all retailers and now to a wide range of digital experience providers like Alphabet and Meta.

Market vs. Industry Point of View: Defining competitors from a market point of view (companies satisfying the same customer need) opens the company’s eyes to a broader set of actual and potential competitors than an industry point of view (firms in the same industry).



9.19.1.2 Assessing Competitors:

Objectives: Understand competitors’ goals (profitability, market share, cash flow, technological leadership, etc.) to predict their reactions.

Strategies (Strategic Groups): Identify “strategic groups” – firms following similar strategies in a target market (e.g., Whirlpool, Maytag, LG in home appliances). Competition is most intense within these groups but can also occur across them (e.g., LG Signature competing with Viking and Sub-Zero).

Strengths and Weaknesses: Gather data on competitors’ performance, conduct market research, and utilize “benchmarking” to compare products and processes against leading firms to identify best practices and areas for improvement.

Reaction Patterns: Understand how competitors are likely to react to company moves (e.g., price cuts, new product launches). P&G, for example, is known for reacting “fiercely” to challenges in its market.



9.19.1.3 Selecting Competitors to Attack and Avoid:

Weak vs. Strong Competitors: Companies often prefer to target weak competitors to conserve resources, but competing with strong rivals can sharpen abilities and yield greater returns.

Customer Value Analysis: A crucial tool to assess competitor strengths and weaknesses. It involves identifying customer-valued attributes, their importance, and comparing the company’s performance against competitors on those attributes. The goal is to “find the place in the market where it meets customers’ needs in a way rivals can’t.”

Good vs. Bad Competitors: “Good competitors” play by industry rules, while “bad competitors” (like Spirit Airlines’ rock-bottom pricing) disrupt the market in ways that can cause headaches for others. Companies actually need competitors to share development costs, legitimize new technologies, and increase total demand (e.g., Volkswagen’s entry into electric cars helps Tesla legitimize the market).

Blue-Ocean Strategy: Instead of head-to-head competition in “red oceans,” companies can create “blue oceans” of uncontested market space by developing products/services with no direct competitors. Apple (iPod, iPhone, App Store), Square (SMB payment processing), and Netflix (subscription DVDs, streaming, original content) are prime examples of this “value innovation.” Direct-to-consumer (DTC) brands also leverage this strategy.




9.19.2 Approaches to Marketing Strategy

9.19.2.1 Marketing strategy approaches evolve through stages:

Entrepreneurial Marketing: Characterized by “wits,” flexible strategies, and direct engagement (e.g., Jim Koch of Boston Beer Company selling Samuel Adams door-to-door).

Formulated Marketing: Companies develop formal strategies and adhere to them.

Intrapreneurial Marketing: Large, mature companies foster internal entrepreneurship and creativity to avoid losing their initial marketing passion (e.g., Google’s Area 120, Facebook’s hackathons).




9.19.3 Basic Competitive Strategies

9.19.3.1 Michael Porter identified three winning strategies:

Overall Cost Leadership: Achieve the lowest production and distribution costs to price lower and gain market share (e.g., Walmart, Spirit Airlines).

Differentiation: Create a highly differentiated product line and marketing program to be a “class leader” (e.g., Nike, Caterpillar).

Focus: Serve a few market segments well (e.g., Ritz-Carlton, Bose, DuckDuckGo).

“Middle-of-the-roaders” that don’t pursue a clear strategy perform poorly.



9.19.3.2 Treacy and Wiersema offer a customer-centered classification of “value disciplines”:

Operational Excellence: Provide superior value through price and convenience, leading the industry in costs and efficient value delivery (e.g., Walmart, IKEA, Lidl).

Customer Intimacy: Precisely segment markets and tailor products/services to match unique needs, building long-term loyalty (e.g., Amazon, Salesforce, Nordstrom).

Product Leadership: Offer a continuous stream of leading-edge products/services, making existing products obsolete (e.g., Apple).

Companies usually excel at one value discipline while meeting industry standards on others.



9.19.4 Competitive Positions and Roles

9.19.4.1 Companies occupy different competitive positions, each requiring a distinct strategy:

Market Leader (40% market share): Has the largest market share, leads in price changes, new product introductions, distribution, and promotion. Must constantly innovate and defend.
