People with high EQs make an average of $29,000 more per year than those with low EQs.

The link between EQ and earnings is so direct that every point increase in EQ adds $1,300 to an annual salary.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
section: 17.4 What Emotional Intelligence Looks Like: Understanding the Four Skills
text:
17.4 What Emotional Intelligence Looks Like: Understanding the Four Skills
The four emotional intelligence skills combine under two primary competencies: Personal Competence (how you manage yourself) and Social Competence (how you manage relationships with others).

17.4.1 1. Self-Awareness (Personal Competence)
Definition: Self-awareness is the ability to accurately perceive your own emotions in the moment and understand your tendencies across situations. It involves developing a straightforward and honest understanding of what makes you tick, including what motivates you and what situations “push your buttons”.
Key Concepts:

Foundational Skill: Self-awareness makes the other three EQ skills easier to use.

Embrace Discomfort: Genuine understanding of emotions requires the willingness to focus on and tolerate uncomfortable or negative feelings.

Emotional Mistakes as Data: Having self-awareness means not being afraid of emotional “mistakes,” as they provide a steady stream of information necessary for growth.








What Self-Awareness Looks Like
What a Lack of Self-Awareness Looks Like




Manages emotions; they don’t manage him.
Stress and urgency are projected onto others.


Remains calm, cool, and collected, even when frustrated.
Comes across as defensive or aggressive due to lack of awareness of tone and language.


Open and authentic at all times.
Overwhelming personality; doesn’t notice when others are annoyed.





17.4.2 2. Self-Management (Personal Competence)
Definition: Self-management is the ability to use your awareness of your emotions to stay flexible and direct your behavior positively. It is revealed not only in resisting explosive behavior but also in the ability to tolerate uncertainty and explore options during difficult emotional situations.
Key Concepts:

Action, or Inaction: Self-management is what happens when you act—or choose not to act.

Delaying Gratification: Real results come from putting momentary needs on hold to pursue larger, more important goals, requiring commitment to self-management over time.

Preventing Hijackings: High self-management prevents emotional hijackings, allowing you to react nimbly to change and take initiative.








What Self-Management Looks Like
What a Lack of Self-Management Looks Like




Epitome of patience and understanding during heated meetings.
Responds too quickly, sharply, or disjointedly when stressed.


Ability to separate emotion from logic during a crisis.
Emotional reactions “trickle” onto teammates, creating panic.


Handles confrontational situations well; never loses cool.
Reveals stress to staff, setting a negative tone rather than diffusing it.





17.4.3 3. Social Awareness (Social Competence)
Definition: Social awareness is your ability to accurately pick up on emotions in other people and understand what is truly going on with them, even if you don’t feel the same way. This often means perceiving what others are thinking and feeling.
Key Concepts:

Outward Focus: Requires looking outward to learn about and appreciate others, tuning into their emotions.

Listening and Observing: These are the most important elements. To excel, you must stop talking, stop your inner monologue, stop anticipating responses, and stop planning what you will say next.

The Anthropologist Approach: Observing others in their natural state without letting your own thoughts or feelings disturb the observation.








What Social Awareness Looks Like
What a Lack of Social Awareness Looks Like




Has a rare talent to read others’ emotions and adjusts to situations.
Needs to allow others to feel good about their ideas, even if their own plan is better.


Good at acknowledging feelings when communicating difficult news.
Hard to get a word in edgewise; talks over others when excited.


Uncanny ability to spot and address the “elephant in the room”.
Misses the non-technical currents and the mood/evolution of opinions in meetings.





17.4.4 4. Relationship Management (Social Competence)
Definition: Relationship management is your ability to use your awareness of your own emotions and those of others to manage interactions successfully, ensuring clear communication and effective handling of conflict. It is the bond built with others over time.
Key Concepts:

Integrated Skill: This skill taps into all three other EQ skills: Self-Awareness (noticing your feelings), Self-Management (expressing feelings productively), and Social Awareness (understanding the other person’s needs).

Frequency and Quality: Relationships are a product of the quality, depth, and time spent interacting with another person.

Handling Conflict: Relationship management provides the skills needed to passively avoid problems or aggressively explode when frustrated.








What Relationship Management Looks Like
What a Lack of Relationship Management Looks Like




Creates a safe and inviting forum for discussion; maintains an “open-door” policy.
If they disagree with someone, they make it clear the relationship isn’t worth developing.


Empathetic listener; remains calm and answers difficult questions without alienating others.
Dismisses others’ thoughts and fails to tailor communication style (e.g., always very direct).


Finds common ground and inquires about others’ interests.
Minimizes a person’s point of view or experience; comes across as blunt and not empathetic.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
section: 17.5 Digging In: My Emotional Intelligence Action Plan
text:
17.5 Digging In: My Emotional Intelligence Action Plan
EQ skills are highly malleable due to the brain’s ability to change, a concept known as plasticity. Developing EQ involves strengthening the neural pathways between the rational and emotional centers of the brain. This process is gradual and requires tremendous effort and repetition until the new behavior becomes a habit. Measurable, lasting changes typically appear three to six months after beginning consistent practice.

17.5.1 Methodology: My EQ Action Plan Steps
To effectively improve EQ, focus your efforts using the following steps:

Transfer Emotional Intelligence Appraisal ® scores onto Part One (My Journey Begins) of the action plan to establish a baseline.

Pick one EQ skill to work on first. The human mind is most effective focusing on one skill at a time. While the Appraisal report recommends a skill, you may choose your own. However, if scores in all four areas are low (below 75), it is recommended not to start with Relationship Management.

Pick three strategies to begin using for your chosen skill, drawing from the strategies chapters.

Choose an EQ mentor. Find someone gifted in your chosen skill and ask them to provide feedback and guidance at regular intervals.

Apply your chosen strategies, keeping these points in mind:

Expect success, not perfection. Pushing yourself means continuing to catch yourself when emotions interfere.

Practice, practice, practice. Quantity of practice in a variety of situations is the secret to increasing EQ.

Be patient. Lasting change takes a few months (3–6 months).


Measure your progress. After sufficient progress, take the Emotional Intelligence Appraisal® a second time to complete Part Two of the action plan and determine the focus for your next skill development efforts.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
text:
17.6 Self-Awareness Strategies
Self-awareness is a continuous journey of understanding the “true essence” of yourself, requiring honesty and courage. Since hard-wired emotional reactions precede rational responses, being aware of the full range of feelings is essential for managing self and relationships.
Key Concepts and Applications (Selected Strategies):
1. Quit Treating Your Feelings as Good or Bad

Concept: Judging emotions (e.g., classifying guilt as “bad”) prevents you from understanding what caused the feeling. Suspending judgment allows the emotion to run its course and vanish.

Application: When an emotion surfaces, immediately notice it, refrain from labeling it good or bad, and remind yourself it is there to communicate something important.

2. Observe the Ripple Effect from Your Emotions

Concept: Your emotional “outpourings” are like a stone dropped in water, sending ripples in all directions and affecting everyone in your vicinity. This is critical because emotions are primary drivers of behavior.

Application: Watch how your emotions impact people immediately, and reflect on how they might affect a wider circle long-term. Ask others how your emotions affect them to understand the ripple effects more clearly.

Lean into Your Discomfort

Concept: The biggest obstacle to self-awareness is avoiding the discomfort that comes from seeing yourself clearly. Ignoring feelings is only a short-term fix; you must move toward, into, and through them to change productively.

Application: Consciously seek out feelings of mild emotional discomfort (boredom, confusion, anticipation). This helps discover personal “arrogances” or things you dismiss that you need to address to avoid repeating unproductive patterns.

Feel Your Emotions Physically

Concept: Emotions trigger physical sensations (e.g., stomach tightening, increased heart rate) due to the mind-body connection. Learning these physical cues allows you to spot emotions before you are mentally aware of them.

Application: Close your eyes and focus on your breathing, heart rate, and muscle tension. Think of positive and negative events and note the distinct physical changes that accompany those emotions.

5. Know Who and What Pushes Your Buttons

Concept: We all have triggers that irritate us. Pinpointing the specific people, situations, or conditions (like noisy offices) that trigger strong emotions allows you to maintain composure.

Application: Create a list of specific people and situations that push your buttons. Dig deeper to discover the source of the button (e.g., why a certain behavior reminds you of a past unresolved conflict) to help manage reactions.

7. Keep a Journal about Your Emotions

Concept: A journal provides objectivity, helping you develop perspective on your emotions and tendencies.

Application: Record events that triggered strong emotions and your response. Note patterns in which emotions get you down or pick you up. Describe the emotions felt daily, including the physical sensations that accompanied them.

14. Seek Feedback

Concept: Your internal lens is tainted by your experiences and moods, making true objectivity impossible. Self-awareness requires knowing yourself from the “inside out and the outside in,” meaning you need others’ perspectives.

Application: Ask for specific feedback from friends, coworkers, or mentors. Look for similarities in the information received, which helps you see how your emotions and reactions affect others.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
text:
17.7 Self-Management Strategies
Self-management is actively choosing what you say and do using emotional awareness. This skill builds directly on self-awareness: you can only choose how to respond productively if you are aware of the underlying feeling. Effective self-management ensures you achieve your goals and do not frustrate others.
Key Concepts and Applications (Selected Strategies):
1. Breathe Right

Concept: Shallow breathing deprives the brain (which uses 20% of the body’s oxygen) of the resources needed for complex functions like managing mood and maintaining focus. Deep, diaphragm breathing (making the stomach protrude) ensures maximum oxygen flow.

Application: During stressful moments, focus on slow, deep breaths, inhaling through the nose until the stomach swells, and exhaling gently through the mouth until the lungs are empty. This immediately engages the rational brain and promotes calmness.

2. Create an Emotion vs. Reason List

Concept: When your emotional mind conflicts with your rational mind over a decision, this tool helps clear your head.

Application (Step-by-Step):

Draw a line down the middle of a page to create two columns.

In the left column, write what your emotions are telling you to do.

In the right column, write what your reason is telling you to do.

Ask: Where are my emotions clouding judgment, and where is my reason ignoring important emotional cues?

Use the list to decide whether the emotional or rational side should guide your decision.


4. Count to Ten

Concept: This simple technique stops rash action and interrupts the flow of anger or frustration, giving the rational brain valuable time to catch up with the overwhelmed emotional brain (limbic system).

Application: When feeling frustrated or angry, take a deep breath and silently count to ten. Even counting a few numbers can cool down the system and allow for a more constructive response.

9. Take Control of Your Self-Talk

Concept: The average person has about 50,000 thoughts daily, and this inner voice (“self-talk”) dictates emotional flow by turning the emotional heat up or down. Negative self-talk is unrealistic and self-defeating.

Application (Reframing Negative Self-Talk):

Turn “I always” or “I never” into “just this time” or “sometimes.”

Accept responsibility for your actions, but not someone else’s burden; similarly, stop always blaming others and take responsibility for your part.


10. Visualize Yourself Succeeding

Concept: Since the brain struggles to distinguish between what you see with your eyes and what you visualize, mental visualization is an effective way to practice new skills and form habits, particularly for situations that don’t occur often.

Application (Step-by-Step):

Find a distraction-free room (before bed is ideal).

Visualize yourself in a challenging situation where you typically lose control.

Concentrate on the sights, sounds, and emotions until you feel them stirring.

Picture yourself acting the way you wish to (e.g., remaining calm, saying the right things). Allow yourself to feel the satisfaction of success.


11. Clean Up Your Sleep Hygiene

Concept: Quality sleep is critical for self-management as patience, flexibility, and alertness are the first things lost when sleep is poor. Good sleep hygiene helps the brain move through necessary cycles to recharge.

Application (Sleep Hygiene Steps):

Get twenty minutes of morning sunlight. This resets the inner clock, aiding evening sleep (must not be filtered by windows or sunglasses).

Turn off the computer at least two hours before bedtime. Screen light tricks the brain, disrupting sleep quality.

Keep your bed for sleeping. Avoid working or watching TV in bed so your body associates the bed with rest.

Avoid caffeine, especially in the p.m. Caffeine has a six-hour half-life and severely disrupts sleep quality.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
text:
17.8 Social Awareness Strategies
Social awareness is achieved by looking outward to appreciate and understand others’ emotional cues, providing a more accurate view of your surroundings. To build this skill, you must ensure your focus is clear, tapping into both your five senses and your “sixth sense” (emotions).
Key Concepts and Applications (Selected Strategies):
1. Greet People by Name

Concept: Using someone’s name is a personal, meaningful, and influential social awareness strategy. It breaks down barriers and comes across as warm and inviting, helping you to remain connected to the person.

Application: Practice using a person’s name at least twice during an introduction or conversation. If a name is unusual, ask the person to spell it to help you remember it later.

2. Watch Body Language

Concept: Body language communicates nonstop and is an abundant source of information about how a person is really feeling. Professionals, like poker players, rely on reading cues (posture, eye contact, gestures) to determine sincerity and confidence.

Application: Perform a head-to-toe assessment: look at eye contact (maintained contact can show sincerity, shifty eyes suggest deception) and smiles (an authentic smile includes a crinkle of skin at the corner of the eyes).

5. Don’t Take Notes at Meetings

Concept: Multi-tasking (like taking notes while listening) sacrifices the quality of work because the brain cannot perform multiple activities at a high level simultaneously. By focusing on writing, you miss critical, non-technical clues like shifts in tone or body language.

Application: At your next meeting, refrain from taking notes. Instead, focus entirely on observing others’ facial expressions and making eye contact with the speaker to gather crucial emotional and social information.

7. Clear Away the Clutter

Concept: Internal distractions (mental chatter, planning responses while others are speaking) crowd out external focus, making social awareness impossible.

Application: When in conversation, do not interrupt until the other person is completely finished. Catch yourself planning a response, stop the thought, and physically lean toward the speaker to refocus on their words and face.

9. Go on a 15-minute Tour

Concept: When focused solely on destinations or tasks, you miss the people between points A and B. Touring your workspace helps you get in tune with others’ emotions and pick up on subtle social clues.

Application: Schedule 15 minutes twice a week to observe your surroundings. Look for the look and feel of workspaces, movement patterns, and who seeks interaction. On another day, tour for moods, noticing the overall atmosphere and how people’s feelings affect you.

11. Practice the Art of Listening

Concept: Listening is not just hearing words, but also analyzing the tone, speed, and volume of the voice for hidden messages. Focus is essential.

Application: Stop everything else when someone talks to you. When on the phone, don’t type an email. When meeting someone, close the door and sit near them to ensure full attention. These simple acts help you pick up on the cues they send.

14. Test for Accuracy

Concept: Even skilled individuals need to validate social observations, especially when there is interference or conflicting cues (e.g., someone says they are “fine” but looks sullen).

Application: Ask reflective questions to clarify observations. For example: “It looks like you are feeling down about something. Did something happen?”. This acknowledges the physical evidence you observe and shows interest.

15. Step into Their Shoes

Concept: Put away your own beliefs, emotions, and thinking patterns to try and experience a situation as the other person would. This is necessary to gain perspective and identify problems before they escalate.

Application: Ask yourself: “If I were this person, how would I respond to this question?” Use your history with them (past reactions, behavior) to answer, not your own default tendencies.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
text:
17.9 Relationship Management Strategies
Relationship management is necessary because all relationships take work. It requires the integration of all four EQ skills: self-awareness (noticing your feelings), self-management (acting accordingly), and social awareness (understanding the other person’s needs). Since you are half of any relationship, you hold half the responsibility for deepening the connection.
Key Concepts and Applications (Selected Strategies):
1. Be Open and Be Curious

Concept: Being open means sharing information about yourself, which helps prevent others from misinterpreting your behavior (e.g., explaining why you are punctual). Being curious means showing interest in the other person’s story to better understand their needs.

Application: Use your self-management skills to choose what information to share about yourself. Ask inquisitive, non-judgmental questions, choosing an appropriate setting and time.

3. Avoid Giving Mixed Signals

Concept: Confusion arises when your words, tone, and body language do not align (e.g., frowning while saying “great job”). People trust what they see over what they hear.

Application: Use self-awareness to identify your current emotions and self-management to decide how to express them. If strong emotions prevent appropriate expression in the moment, put the feeling on the back burner or explain the discrepancy (e.g., “If I seem distracted, it’s because I can’t stop worrying…”).

5. Take Feedback Well

Concept: Feedback is a unique gift for improvement, but the element of surprise can catch us off guard. Receiving feedback gracefully involves actively using self-awareness, social awareness, and self-management.

Application (Steps):

Listen actively using social awareness, asking clarifying questions and asking for specific examples.

Thank the person for their willingness to share, regardless of whether you agree.

Use self-management to delay action. Take time to absorb the point and sort out your feelings before deciding what steps to take.

Follow up with action to show you value their comments and solidify the relationship.


6. Build Trust

Concept: Trust takes time to build, can be lost instantly, and is critical for deepening connections. It is built through open communication, consistency in words and actions, reliability, and willingness to share.

Application: Be the first to lay some of yourself on the line (use self-awareness/self-management to share parts of yourself). Use social awareness to ask the other person what needs to happen to build their trust, and listen intently to their answer.

8. Only Get Mad on Purpose

Concept: Anger is a useful emotion if managed properly. To master it, you must be angry “with the right person, to the right degree, at the right time, for the right purpose, and in the right way”. Anger used purposefully can motivate others or communicate the gravity of a situation.

Application: Define your varying degrees of anger (from annoyed to rage) and write down specific examples. Determination when showing anger will actually improve the relationship, using social awareness to consider the other person’s likely response.

13. Explain Your Decisions, Don’t Just Make Them

Concept: When decisions are made without context, people feel “in the dark” and anxious. To support an idea, people need to understand the rationale. Transparency builds trust and makes people feel respected.

Application: When announcing a change, explain the “why” behind the decision, including alternatives considered, and acknowledge how the decision will affect everyone. Use social awareness to anticipate how the decision will be received.

15. Align Your Intention with Your Impact

Concept: A misalignment occurs when a good intention (e.g., trying to lighten the mood with a joke) results in a negative impact (offense or silence). These misunderstandings tarnish relationships.

Application (Analysis):

Describe an incident, your intentions, your actions, and the resulting impact.

Write down what you did not realize in the situation (missed cues, what you learned about others).

Determine what you could have done differently to ensure your actions matched your positive intentions.


17. Tackle a Tough Conversation (Methodology)

Concept: Tough conversations are inevitable but can be navigated calmly by structuring the discussion and actively managing your reactions.

Steps:

Start with agreement. Find common ground (e.g., agreeing the discussion is important, or a shared goal).

Ask the person to help you understand his or her side. People need to feel heard. Focus on understanding their perspective to prevent frustration from rising.

Resist the urge to plan a “comeback” or a rebuttal. Silence your inner voice (self-management) and direct attention fully to the person.

Communicate clearly and simply. Avoid speaking in circles or code, especially when delivering difficult feedback.

Move the conversation forward. Once perspectives are understood, reiterate common ground and discuss next steps for development or resolution (e.g., “I’m still invested in your development…”).

Keep in touch. Check progress frequently to show continued investment in the relationship.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
section: 17.10 Just the Facts: A Look at the Latest Discoveries in Emotional Intelligence
text:
17.10 Just the Facts: A Look at the Latest Discoveries in Emotional Intelligence
Research conducted by TalentSmart® reveals several key trends regarding EQ scores across demographics and circumstances.

17.10.1 The Poles Are Melting: EQ Then and Now
Emotional intelligence is a skill set that can be learned—and unlearned.

Increase: Between 2003 and 2007, the collective EQ of the U.S. workforce steadily increased, demonstrating that the discussion and intentional practice of emotions help people become more skilled at identifying and understanding them.

Decrease (2008): In 2008, collective EQ scores dropped, linked directly to the economic recession and the resulting stress. Stress taxes mental resources, causing EQ skills to desert people when they are needed most.



17.10.2 The Battle of the Sexes: EQ and Gender

Self-Awareness: Men and women are statistically tied in their ability to recognize their own emotions.

Other Skills: Historically, women outperformed men in self-management, social awareness, and relationship management. However, men have recently caught up in self-management, a change attributed to shifting social norms that encourage men to pay more attention to their emotions.



17.10.3 It’s Lonely at the Top: EQ and Job Title

Trend: EQ scores tend to climb from entry-level positions upward toward middle management.

The Dip: Beyond middle management (Director level and above), there is a steep downward trend in EQ scores.

CEOs: CEOs, on average, have the lowest EQ scores in the workplace. This is often because leaders are promoted based on technical knowledge or tenure rather than on skills for managing people. Nonetheless, among executives, those with the highest EQ scores are the best performers.



17.10.4 The Generational Divide: EQ and Age

Trend: Self-management skills increase steadily with age (e.g., 60-year-olds scored higher than 50-year-olds).

Implication: Younger generations (Gen Y/X) often show deficient self-management skills not because of their upbringing but because they have had less time to practice managing their emotions. They have the capacity to soak up new information and can accelerate their EQ development through conscious effort, allowing them to lead like seasoned veterans sooner.



17.10.5 China’s Secret Weapon: EQ and Culture

Finding: Chinese executives averaged 15 points higher than American executives in self-management and relationship management.

Discipline: Executives in both countries show similar awareness of emotions (self-awareness and social awareness), but Chinese executives are better at putting that awareness into productive action. This advantage is linked to a culture where emotional outbursts and careless self-gratification are personally shameful, promoting emotionally intelligent behaviors like seeking feedback and following through on commitments.

title: 17  Emotional Intelligence 2.0
text:
17.11 Final Comprehensive Summary
Emotional Intelligence 2.0 establishes EQ as a dynamic skill set, crucial for high performance and success, distinct from fixed intellect (IQ) and stable behavioral style (Personality). The book’s core framework revolves around four interconnected skills:

Self-Awareness: Understanding your emotions in the moment, achieved through strategies like journaling, seeking external feedback, and leaning into emotional discomfort.

Self-Management: Using that awareness to direct behavior positively, often requiring the delay of immediate needs for greater goals. Practical tools include deep breathing (to oxygenate the brain) and creating an Emotion vs. Reason List.

Social Awareness: Accurately perceiving and understanding the moods and motivations of others, demanding total focus on listening and observing non-verbal cues (e.g., watching body language, avoiding note-taking in meetings).

Relationship Management: Successfully managing interactions and building lasting bonds by applying the three foundational skills. Key methodologies include taking feedback well, explaining decisions instead of merely making them, and strategically using emotion (“Only Get Mad on Purpose”).

The book emphasizes that EQ is highly susceptible to change—it can be developed through consistent practice (plasticity) but can also deteriorate under stress. The Emotional Intelligence Action Plan provides a structured way to measure progress, choose an improvement focus, and employ strategies repeatedly for lasting behavioral change. Success, both professional and personal, belongs to those who maximize this unique blend of reason and feeling.

title: 18  Getting Things Done
text:
18.1 Executive Summary
The Getting Things Done (GTD) methodology is a comprehensive, five-step system for achieving stress-free productivity. It is founded on the principle that the mind is for having ideas, not for holding them. To achieve a state of relaxed, focused control, all things considered unfinished—“stuff”—must be captured in a trusted system outside the mind.
The core framework involves processing everything that comes into one’s life through five distinct steps: Capture, Clarify, Organize, Reflect, and Engage. This process transforms ambiguous commitments into clearly defined desired outcomes (projects) and concrete next actions. A critical component of this framework is the identification of the “next physical, visible activity” required to move a commitment forward. This ensures that when time and energy allow, one can immediately choose an action without needing further deliberation.
Key operational standards include:
By consistently applying the GTD framework, individuals gain coherence across all their activities (horizontal control) and manage the successful development of individual topics and projects (vertical control), leading to clarity, confidence, and increased capacity for higher-level thinking (Horizons 3–5).

title: 18  Getting Things Done
text:
The Two-Minute Rule: If defining an action takes less than two minutes, do it immediately when it is defined, as it takes longer to track it than to complete it.

Next-Action Decision Making: Every commitment must have a defined next action; without this decision, items remain stuck (procrastinated).

The Weekly Review: This crucial practice ensures the system stays current, complete, and trusted, serving as the “master key” to maintaining focused control.

title: 18  Getting Things Done
section: 18.2 The Art of Getting Things Done
text:
18.2 The Art of Getting Things Done

18.2.1 A New Practice for a New Reality
This chapter introduces the fundamental premise that traditional time-management models are insufficient for handling the volume and complexity of modern knowledge work. Work today often lacks clear edges or definitions of completion.

18.2.1.1 Key Concepts
The Need for Relaxed Control and Full Presence It is possible to function productively with a clear head and a positive sense of relaxed control, a state critical for successful professionals. This ideal state allows one to be fully present—whether working, socializing, or relaxing—with confidence that whatever is being done is exactly what should be done at that moment. The core methods presented aim to leverage existing basic skills (writing things down, deciding outcomes and actions, making choices) into new levels of effectiveness.
The Failure of Old Models Traditional organizational models, such as using calendars, daily to-do lists, and simplified priority coding (like “ABC” priorities), are inadequate for managing the high volume and variability of the modern workload. Similarly, focusing solely on the “bigger view” (values and major goals) fails if the day-to-day commitments are distracting or overwhelming, creating subconscious resistance to taking on larger, complex goals.
The Missing Piece: Action Management The key to managing “stuff” (anything unfinished or undetermined) is not managing time, information, or priorities, but managing actions. Things get stuck not because of a lack of time, but because the next physical step, or what “doing” looks like, hasn’t been decided.
Horizontal and Vertical Action Management Effective workflow requires two types of control:

Horizontal Control: Maintaining coherence across the breadth of all activities and commitments in one’s life (e.g., managing the transition between work, family, social life, etc.). A good system must track many items and allow focus to shift quickly.

Vertical Control: Managing thinking, development, and coordination within individual topics or projects. This is “project planning” in a broad sense, fleshing out ideas, details, and sequences required for a single endeavor.

The Major Change: Getting It All Out of Your Head There is an inverse relationship between how much something is on your mind and how much it is getting done. The goal is to capture and organize 100% of “stuff” in objective tools, not relying on the mind for storage. The short-term memory (like a computer’s RAM) has limited capacity, and incomplete items stored there reduce focus and performance.


18.2.1.2 Step-by-Step Breakdown: Dealing with Internal Commitments
Managing commitments requires specific activities and behaviors:

Capture: Anything considered unfinished must be captured in a trusted collection tool outside the mind.

Clarify: Determine exactly what the commitment is and decide the next action, if any, to make progress.

Organize: Keep reminders of all required actions organized in a system reviewed regularly.







Practical Application: The Essence of Stuff




Stuff is anything allowed into your psychological or physical world that doesn’t belong where it is, but for which the desired outcome and next action have not been determined.


Stuff transforms into a clear inventory of meaningful actions, projects, and usable information once clarified.


The goal is to apply concentrated thinking to define desired outcomes and requisite next actions.




Recap

Traditional time management fails due to the volume and complexity of modern “knowledge work”.

The highest performance state is achieved through “mind like water”—relaxed, focused control.

Anxiety is caused by a lack of control, organization, preparation, and action.

The mind’s short-term memory (RAM) is not a storage place; relying on it for remembering incomplete tasks creates psychological load.

Managing all commitments requires clarity on the outcome, defining the next physical action, and tracking reminders in a trusted system.

Productivity comes from managing actions, not time, information, or priorities.

A bottom-up approach (getting control of the mundane) is often more effective than a top-down approach (vision setting).

Horizontal control manages the breadth of activities; vertical control manages project depth.




18.2.2 Getting Control of Your Life: The Five Steps of Mastering Workflow
Mastering workflow involves five discrete stages that must be executed consistently and separately, as the quality of the workflow management is only as good as the weakest link. Trying to execute all five steps simultaneously is a major reason people fail to get organized.

18.2.2.1 Framework: The Five Steps of Mastering Workflow
The horizontal management of one’s life is accomplished by moving through these phases:

Capture: Collect all items that have your attention.

Clarify: Define what each item means and what to do about it.

Organize: Place the results into the correct categories and compartments.

Reflect: Review the system contents to assess options.

Engage: Execute the chosen actions.




18.2.2.2 1. Capture: Corralling Your Stuff
Core Concept: The goal is to eliminate “holes in your bucket” by collecting placeholders for 100 percent of the “incompletes”—anything, big or small, personal or professional, that you are internally committed to changing or deciding about. If it’s not in your trusted system, it is still resident in your mental space.
Methodology: Capturing Successfully

Get It All Out of Your Head: You must believe that you have truly captured everything for your mind to let go of the task of remembering.

Minimize the Number of Capture Locations: Use as many collection tools (in-trays, pads, digital devices) as you need to be ubiquitous, but as few as possible to make processing manageable.

Empty the Capture Tools Regularly: Collection tools must be emptied and processed promptly, otherwise they cease to function effectively.




18.2.2.3 2. Clarify: Processing Your Incompletes
Core Concept: Clarifying is the process of deciding the meaning of each captured item. This involves determining if an item is actionable and, if so, what the desired outcome and next action are.
The Clarifying Workflow (Processing “In” to Empty) For every item captured, ask:

What is it? Identify the item or commitment.

Is it actionable? Is something required to move this forward?

If No Action Required:

Trash: Throw it away, shred it, or recycle it.

Incubate: Put it on hold for later reassessment (Someday/Maybe list or tickler file).

Reference: File it so it can be retrieved later.


If Actionable (Yes):

What is the desired outcome (Project)? If more than one step is required, define the desired result (the project) and capture it on a Projects list.

What is the Next Action? Identify the next physical, visible activity required to move the item toward completion.



Next Action Decision: Do It, Delegate It, or Defer It

Do It: If the action takes less than two minutes (The Two-Minute Rule), do it immediately. This is the efficiency cutoff.

Delegate It: If the action takes longer than two minutes, ask “Am I the right person to do this?” If no, delegate it to the appropriate entity and place a reminder on a Waiting For list.

Defer It: If the action takes longer than two minutes and you are the right person to do it, defer it and track it on appropriate Next Actions lists.




18.2.2.4 3. Organize: Setting Up the Right Buckets
Core Concept: Organizing means that where something is located matches what it means to you. This step involves setting up the “right buckets”—the categories for reminders and materials resulting from the clarification process. These categories must be pristinely distinct from one another.
The Eight Organizational Categories

Projects List: An index of every desired result requiring more than one action step to complete, committed to within a year. This list is primarily used for regular review, not daily execution.

Project Support Material: Details, plans, and supporting information related to active projects, kept in separate folders, binders, or digital files.

Calendar: Holds only time-specific actions (appointments), day-specific actions (like a call that must happen Tuesday), and day-specific information (like event directions or due dates). Crucially, it should not hold general daily to-do lists.

Next Actions Lists: Holds all deferred actions categorized by the context required to perform them (e.g., location, tool, or person needed).

Common Categories: Calls, At Computer, Errands, At Office (miscellaneous), At Home, Anywhere, Agendas (for specific people or meetings), Read/Review (for longer reading tasks).


Waiting For List: A list tracking delegated tasks or expected items from others, along with the date they were delegated or requested.

Reference Material: Nonactionable information (paper or digital) kept for retrieval, organized by topic or area. The core tool is a fast, functional, and simple general-reference filing system (often single A–Z).

Someday/Maybe List: A repository for ideas, projects, and fantasies that currently have no commitment to action, but which one may want to pursue in the future (e.g., “Learn Spanish,” “Get a sailboat”).

Trash: Items to be discarded.




18.2.2.5 4. Reflect: Keeping the System Functional
Core Concept: Reflection is the act of stepping back and reviewing the system contents to keep the entire inventory—and one’s awareness—current and functional. This prevents the mind from taking back the job of remembering.
The Critical Success Factor: The Weekly Review The Weekly Review is the nonnegotiable practice necessary to maintain psychological equilibrium and trust in the system. It ensures that all commitments are assessed frequently enough to stay off the mind.
The Weekly Review Steps (Three-Part Drill)

Get Clear (Processing Loose Ends):

Collect loose papers and materials (from pockets, desks, devices).

Get “In” to empty (process all notes, e-mails, and inputs to zero).

Empty your head (capture any remaining thoughts as new actions/projects).


Get Current (Updating Maps):

Review Next Actions lists (mark off completions and add new next steps).

Review previous calendar data (capture remaining actions/info).

Review upcoming calendar (identify required preparation actions).

Review Waiting For list (follow up as needed).

Review Projects list (ensure every project has a current next action).

Review relevant checklists (including Areas of Focus).


Get Creative (Generating New Ideas):

Review Someday/Maybe list (pull items forward into active projects if motivated).

Review Goals and Vision (Horizons 3–5) to generate new ideas, projects, and commitments.





18.2.2.6 5. Engage: Making the Best Action Choices
Core Concept: Engaging is the execution phase—choosing the optimal action in the moment. If the previous four steps are maintained, action choices can be made intuitively, based on current reality.
Choosing Actions in the Moment: The Four-Criteria Model

Context: What location, people, or tools are available to you right now? (e.g., At Phone, At Computer, At Home).

Time Available: How much time do you have before your next commitment? Use “weird time” (small, random windows of time) productively by matching actions to available minutes.

Energy Available: How much mental or physical energy do you have? Match high-energy tasks with high-energy times, and use low-energy times for simple tasks (e.g., filing, reading, organizing).

Priority: Given the first three constraints, what action from your lists is the most important to accomplish?.







Application: The Threefold Model for Daily Work




1. Doing Predefined Work: Working from your Next Actions lists and Calendar (executing tasks previously clarified).


2. Doing Work as It Shows Up: Handling unexpected actions, interruptions, or crises, including two-minute tasks (This is often reactive).


3. Defining Your Work: The process of capturing and clarifying new inputs, organizing the results, and breaking down projects.




Recap

The GTD framework is a five-step continuous process: Capture, Clarify, Organize, Reflect, and Engage.

Clarifying involves determining if an item is actionable (resulting in a next action or project) or nonactionable (resulting in trash, incubation, or reference).

The Two-Minute Rule dictates immediate action to maintain efficiency.

Deferred actions are organized by context (e.g., @Calls, @Errands) to ensure reminders appear when they can be acted upon.

A Project is any desired outcome requiring more than one step to complete.

The Calendar is sacred territory, reserved only for time- or day-specific obligations, not general to-dos.

The Weekly Review is essential for maintaining trust, cleanliness, and completeness of the entire system.

Engagement involves choosing actions based on context, time, energy, and priority.




18.2.3 Getting Projects Creatively Under Way: The Five Phases of Project Planning
While horizontal focus (the five steps of workflow) is often sufficient, complex projects require structured, creative thought—vertical focus. This informal planning is often called “back-of-the-envelope planning”.

18.2.3.1 Framework: The Natural Planning Model
The human brain naturally follows a five-step process whenever it seeks to execute a desired outcome:

Defining Purpose and Principles: Clarifying the “Why”.

Purpose: The intended function or goal (e.g., satisfying hunger, signing a business deal). Clear purpose creates decision-making criteria, aligns resources, and expands creative options.

Principles: The standards, values, or behaviors that define the boundaries of the plan (e.g., affordability, quality, ethical conduct).


Outcome Visioning: Imagining the successful result.

This is the “What”—a clear mental picture of what success will look, sound, and feel like. This focus affects perception and performance, acting as a filter for relevant information (like a computer’s search function).


Brainstorming: Generating all potential ideas, details, and components related to the vision.

Rules for effective brainstorming: Don’t judge, challenge, evaluate, or criticize; go for quantity, not quality; put analysis and organization in the background. Capturing these ideas externally (like mind mapping or listing) prevents the mind from having to hold them.


Organizing: Structuring the gathered ideas by components, sequences, and/or priorities.

This is where natural structure emerges from the raw brainstormed data. The result often involves hierarchical outlines or flowcharts.


Identifying Next Actions: Determining the specific physical steps required to move the project forward, for every active component.



18.2.3.2 Application: When to Plan More Deeply
Most projects require only in-head planning to identify the next action. The Natural Planning Model (NPM) should be deliberately applied when a project is new, stuck, or ambiguous.

Need More Clarity? Shift thinking up the planning scale. If brainstorming is fuzzy, re-examine the outcome/vision; if the outcome is unclear, return to the core purpose.

Need More to Be Happening (Action)? Shift thinking down the planning scale. If there is enthusiasm about the purpose but no action, the vision may be unclear. If brainstorming is done but action is stuck, rigor is required to organize deliverables and define next actions.


Recap

Vertical focus (project planning) is necessary for complex situations.

The Natural Planning Model (NPM) mirrors the brain’s instinctual process for achieving outcomes.

The five phases of NPM are Purpose, Vision, Brainstorming, Organizing, and Next Actions.

Purpose (the “Why”) defines criteria and aligns resources.

Vision (the “What”) focuses the mind’s resources to generate solutions (teleological function).

Brainstorming should prioritize quantity of ideas and suspend judgment.

Projects are sufficiently planned when every active component has a next action assigned.

If a project is still on your mind, more thinking (planning) is required.

title: 18  Getting Things Done
text:
18.3 Practicing Stress-Free Productivity

18.3.1 Getting Started: Setting Up the Time, Space, and Tools
Successful implementation of the GTD methodology is supported by setting up the physical and digital environment optimally.

18.3.1.1 Setting Up Time and Space

Time Commitment: An ideal initial implementation takes two whole, contiguous days. This minimizes interruption, which can double the time needed for the intensive front-end cognitive work of capturing and clarifying.

Work Environment: A dedicated, individual, self-contained workspace (at home, at work, and even in transit) is critical.

The basics require a writing surface, room for an in-tray, and core digital tools.

Most professionals need sufficient filing space (at least two drawers) for general reference and project support materials.


Office in Transit: An efficiently organized micro-office (briefcase/pack) is needed to take advantage of short “weird time” windows and moments between locations.



18.3.1.2 Step-by-Step Breakdown: Essential Tools
The necessary tools are either low-tech (paper/physical) or high-tech (digital).








Tool Category
Essential Items
Success Factors/Notes




Physical Processing
Paper-holding trays (at least three), plain letter-size paper, Post-its, clips, stapler, tape, rubber bands.
Trays should be stackable and side-facing for easy retrieval. Plain paper is used for capturing one thought/idea per sheet for individual processing.


Organization/Tracking
An automatic labeler, file folders, a calendar, wastebasket/recycling bins.
The Labeler is a “surprisingly useful tool” that makes filing easy and attractive. The calendar holds the “hard landscape” of commitments—things that must happen.


Reference System
General-reference files (physical and digital) are crucial. The filing system must be fast, functional, and fun. If it takes longer than one minute to file, it will be resisted.
Recommended: A single A–Z system for general reference to minimize ambiguity about where something is filed. File drawers should be kept less than three-quarters full to reduce unconscious resistance to filing.




Recap

Implementation works best with an initial block of uninterrupted time (ideally two days).

Setting up a dedicated, functional workspace (the “home base”) minimizes unconscious resistance to processing.

A dedicated general-reference filing system is critical to prevent nonactionable clutter from clogging the workflow.

A single A-Z filing system is recommended for general reference to simplify searching.

The automatic labeler should be treated as a basic, indispensable tool.

A key operational trick is to “Put It in Front of the Door”—using an external trigger to ensure important items are remembered at the right time.




18.3.2 Capturing: Corralling Your “Stuff”
This chapter details the physical and mental gathering process necessary to achieve the “empty head” state.

18.3.2.1 Step-by-Step Breakdown: The Capture Process
The capture phase is complete only when one can easily see the outer edges of the inventory of everything that has attention.

Preparation: Before starting, handle any urgent existing commitments so the mind is free to focus entirely on the capture task.

Boundary Definition: Identify items that can stay where they are because they require no action: Supplies, Reference Material, Decoration, and Equipment. Everything else needs to be captured.

Physical Gathering: Systematically move through all locations (desktop, drawers, countertops, files, shelves, etc.).

If an item is too large (e.g., a piece of equipment), write a note to represent it and put the note into the in-tray.

If a stack is too large for the in-tray, create stacks around it, clearly delineating them as “in”.

Avoid the “Purge-and-Organize Bug”—the primary goal is capturing, not cleaning or organizing, which happens later in the process.


Mental Gathering (Mind Sweep): Once the physical environment is clear, capture all remaining items lingering in your mental space.

Use plain paper to write out each thought, idea, or project on a separate sheet. This creates discrete placeholders for later processing.

Use the Incompletion Triggers list (covering professional, personal, financial, planning, systems, meetings, and waiting for) to jog the memory and ensure completeness [307–310].


Final Inventory: The final inventory of “in” includes all physical papers/notes, voice mails (transferred to paper notes), task lists (printed out), and digital messages staged in the communication software “in” area.







Note on Stuff and Attention




Resistance to Capture: People resist capturing items because they often seem “not that important,” but these items still nag at the consciousness and take up disproportionate energy.


Pack Rat vs. Action Management: The GTD method is not necessarily advocacy for minimalism. One can keep twelve boxes of old journals, provided they are where they want them to be and any required actions about them (e.g., “organize journals”) are captured and processed.




Recap

The Capture phase should include every single incomplete commitment, personally and professionally, to ensure the mind fully trusts the external system.

Items requiring no action (supplies, reference, decoration, equipment) should remain in place.

If an item is too big for the in-tray, a note representing it should be captured instead.

The “Mind Sweep” is used to extract lurking mental commitments, with each thought written on a separate note.

The goal is to get everything into the funnel quickly, postponing in-depth clarification and organizing until the next step.




18.3.3 Clarifying: Getting “In” to Empty
This is the central process of deciding what an item is and what to do about it. It requires fast, hard thinking about the next action.

18.3.3.1 Processing Guidelines

Process the top item first: Start at one end and move in order. Avoid emergency scanning or picking and choosing items, as this undermines the system’s integrity.

Process one item at a time: Do not let the eyes wander to other items. Focused attention forces the decision needed to move forward.

Never put anything back into “in”: Once an item is touched, it must be determined what it means and where it needs to go, even if the decision is to defer action.



18.3.3.2 Step-by-Step Breakdown: The Clarifying Process

Trash/Reference/Incubate: If the item is nonactionable, dispose of it, file it in the reference system, or stage it for future review (incubate).

Determine Next Action: If the item is actionable, identify the next physical, visible activity required to move the situation toward closure.

Example: For “Clean the garage,” the next action might be “Call John re: refrigerator in garage”.

If the next step is “deciding what to do,” this is not an action. There is always a preceding physical activity (e.g., “Draft ideas for the decision,” or “Call Fred to get input”) to facilitate the decision.


Execute the 3 D’s: Decide whether to Do it, Delegate it, or Defer it.







Methodology: The Two-Minute Rule




Rule: If an action will take less than two minutes, it should be done at the moment it is defined.


Rationale: It’s more efficient to do the task now than to spend time capturing, clarifying, organizing, and reflecting on it later.


Flexibility: The two-minute benchmark is a guideline. In focused processing sessions, it may be extended to five or ten minutes. When rushing, it might be shortened.




Tracking Delegation: If delegated, the item must be tracked on a Waiting For list. The date of delegation should be recorded, as follow-up responsibility now rests with the delegator.

Tracking Deferral: If the action is the user’s to do and takes longer than two minutes, it is deferred to the appropriate Calendar (if time-sensitive) or Next Actions list (if as-soon-as-possible).

Identifying Projects: After identifying next actions, check if the defined action will complete the commitment. If more than one step is required, define the desired result as a Project (outcome requiring more than one action step) and add it to the Projects list.


Recap

Processing requires focused effort, moving through items one at a time from top to bottom.

The core difficulty in clarifying is determining the absolute next physical step.

The next action decision releases mental energy by creating a concrete, achievable task instead of an overwhelming, amorphous goal.

The two-minute rule should be applied primarily when engaging with new input.

Delegated tasks must be tracked on the Waiting For list to manage the commitment of others.

The process culminates in generating the Projects list, ensuring every commitment has a defined outcome.




18.3.4 Organizing: Setting Up the Right Buckets
Airtight organization requires that categories are kept pristinely distinct (hard edges). The organizational system is composed entirely of lists and folders.

18.3.4.1 Organizing Action Reminders
Actions are grouped by the most appropriate reminder structure: Calendar (for fixed time/day) or Next Actions Lists (for as-soon-as-possible).







Category
Purpose and Application




Calendar
For time-specific actions (appointments) and day-specific actions/information (e.g., directions, due dates, or reminders that must surface on a certain day).


Next Actions Lists (Context-Based)
The best way to be reminded of an “as soon as I can” action is by the particular context required. Sorting by context maximizes productivity by leveraging windows of time and focused energy (e.g., consolidating all calls).


Agendas Lists
Running lists of items to discuss with specific people (bosses, partners, assistants, advisors) or in standing meetings. This minimizes interruptions and maximizes communication efficiency.


Read/Review
For items requiring more than two minutes of reading time. Physical materials are often managed in a separate tray; digital content is managed in a separate digital folder.


Waiting For List
Inventory of delegated or expected items. This must be reviewed regularly to follow up on the commitments of others.





18.3.4.2 Organizing Nonactionable Data (Reference and Incubation)
Nonactionable items fall into three categories: Trash, Reference, and Incubation.

Reference Materials: Information kept for later use.

Goal: To have a simple library of data, easily retrievable.

Systems: General-reference filing (single A-Z system for paper and ad hoc digital items); Large-category filing (for complex topics needing 50+ folders); Contact managers.

Principle: Once distinguished from actionable items, reference material adds no psychological weight, allowing one to keep as much as space permits.


Incubation: Items staged for later reassessment.

Someday/Maybe List: Inventory of projects/ideas that are optional or not currently actionable (e.g., hobby ideas, future trips). Reviewing this list periodically is crucial for harnessing creative imaginings.

Tickler File (Bring-Forward File): A 43-folder system (31 days, 12 months) used to hold physical reminders (or notes) that need to surface on a specific future date. This is a unique executive function: deciding not to decide until a last responsible moment.








Methodology: Setting Up a Physical Tickler File




1. Acquire 43 file folders: 31 labeled 1-31 (days), 12 labeled with months.


2. Arrange the folders in a container:


3. Place the daily files in front, starting with the next day’s date, until the 31st.


4. Place the 12 monthly files behind the daily files, starting with the next month.


Daily Routine: Every day, check the daily file. The contents are pulled out and processed in the in-tray. The emptied file is moved to the back, labeled with the following month’s correct date.





18.3.4.3 Application: Checklists
Checklists are “recipes of potential ingredients” used to organize procedures, evaluate potential steps, or ensure nothing is missed in an area of responsibility.

Purpose: To extend the number of important operations one can perform without thinking, conserving mental energy.

Application: Used for blueprints of work (e.g., Job Areas of Responsibility), regular maintenance procedures (e.g., Weekly Review steps), or in novel situations where control is required.

Principle: They provide structure until a procedure becomes automatic (like training wheels), allowing the mind to focus on exceptions rather than routines.


Recap

Organization hinges on separating action from nonaction and keeping categories distinct.

The next-action decision is crucial, as it determines which context-based list receives the reminder.

Next actions should be grouped by context (tool, location, or person) to maximize efficiency when one is in that mode.

A single A-Z general-reference system is preferred over multiple project-based filing systems for flexibility.

Incubation stages non-urgent ideas (Someday/Maybe list) or date-specific reminders (Tickler File).

The Tickler File is a low-tech “personal postal service” for ensuring physical items surface on a future date.

Checklists document procedures and areas of accountability, supporting focus and allowing operations to advance without constant deliberation.




18.3.5 Reflecting: Keeping It All Fresh and Functional
Consistent reflection is the prerequisite for clarity and stability.

18.3.5.1 What to Look At, When

Calendar: Most frequently reviewed item. This handles the “hard landscape” of the day’s fixed commitments.

Next Action Lists: Reviewed whenever one has discretionary time and the corresponding context (e.g., access to a phone).

Projects, Waiting For, and Someday/Maybe Lists: Reviewed only as often as needed to prevent the user from wondering about their status. For most people, this means during the Weekly Review.



18.3.5.2 Step-by-Step Breakdown: The Weekly Review (Expanded)
The Weekly Review ensures that all three requirements for relaxed control are met: all “in” is processed, all systems are updated, and new creative ideas are captured.
1. Get Clear

Collect all loose papers, notes, and receipts from all locations (pockets, briefcase, digital inboxes) into the in-tray.

Process all “In” to empty (notes, e-mails, etc.), applying the clarifying process to each item.

Empty Your Head (mind sweep) to capture any remaining commitments not yet externalized.

2. Get Current

Review Next Actions Lists (check off completed, identify new next steps for old items).

Review Previous Calendar (scan last 2-3 weeks for missed actions or information, and ensure the calendar is archive-ready).

Review Upcoming Calendar (look ahead for preparations needed for upcoming meetings, travel, or deadlines).

Review Waiting For List (follow up on stagnant items or check off received items).

Review Projects List (check status of each project, ensuring a next action is defined for all active projects).

Review Checklists (Roles, Goals, etc.).

3. Get Creative

Review Someday/Maybe List (pull forward items that are now relevant or exciting).

Review Horizons of Focus (Horizons 3–5) to ensure alignment and trigger new objectives or projects.



18.3.5.3 Practical Application: Scheduling the Review
The Weekly Review is highly effective because it mimics the pre-vacation clear-out.

Ideal Time: Block out two hours early in the afternoon of the last workday (e.g., Friday).

Rationale: The week’s events are fresh, and there is still time to reach colleagues for necessary actions before the weekend.

Customization: The review time should be tailored to one’s personal lifestyle (e.g., long commute, Saturday morning coffee shop).


Recap

Consistent reviewing is required for the mind to trust the external system and remain clear.

Most day-to-day review is an accumulation of brief moments of checking the calendar and context-based Next Actions lists.

The Weekly Review is the master key for maintaining system integrity and keeping up with the “whirlwind of activity”.

The three components of the review are Get Clear, Get Current, and Get Creative.

During the review, the Projects List is scanned to ensure a next action is defined for everything in motion.

Reviewing the Someday/Maybe list is the primary component of the “Get Creative” phase.

The ideal time for the Weekly Review is typically two hours on a Friday afternoon.




18.3.6 Engaging: Making the Best Action Choices
Engaging is the process of making appropriate choices about what to do at any point in time, supported by three different priority frameworks.

18.3.6.1 1. The Four-Criteria Model (In-the-Moment)
This model, is used for choosing which task to execute from the Next Actions lists.

Context: Defines what can possibly be done where you are, with the tools you have. Organizing lists by context automatically grounds actions in reality.

Time Available: Match actions to the precise window of time available. Using “weird time” productively requires lists to include short, simple actions.

Energy Available: Match actions to current vitality level. Use low-energy times for non-demanding tasks (e.g., filing, organizing, light reading).

Priority: After the first three constraints are considered, use the remaining two frameworks (Threefold and Six-Level Models) to determine the relative importance of the remaining options.



18.3.6.2 2. The Threefold Model for Evaluating Daily Work
This defines the three kinds of activities competing for attention on a daily basis:

Doing Predefined Work: Working through context-based Next Actions lists and the Calendar.

Doing Work as It Shows Up: Handling unplanned interruptions, communications, or crises. There are no interruptions—only mismanaged inputs; if the system is current, responding to surprises is a conscious choice.

Defining Your Work: Processing the in-tray, clarifying new inputs, and breaking down projects.



18.3.6.3 3. The Six-Level Model for Reviewing Your Own Work
This provides the hierarchical perspective (altitude) necessary to assign priority and meaning to actions. Priorities flow from the top down.








Horizon Level (Altitude)
Focus & Definition
Application & Review Cycle




Horizon 5: Purpose and Principles
Ultimate intention, core values, and raison d’être.
Ongoing criteria for monitoring and correcting activities. Why does your organization/life exist?.


Horizon 4: Vision
Long-term (3–5 year) desired outcomes and ideal scenarios.
Reviewed as often as needed to ensure the immediate work is pointing in the right direction.


Horizon 3: Goals
Mid- to long-term (1–2 year) objectives and outcomes.
Provides criteria for assessing life direction and setting new projects.


Horizon 2: Areas of Focus/Accountabilities
Roles and responsibilities that must be maintained at a certain standard (e.g., staff development, health, finances).
Used as a checklist to ensure all necessary commitments have associated projects. Reviewed every 1–3 months.


Horizon 1: Current Projects
All outcomes requiring more than one action step to complete within the next year.
The core of the Weekly Review, ensuring a next action is active for every commitment.


Ground: Current Actions
The accumulated inventory of all next actions (on context lists).
Reviewed daily, moment-to-moment, using the Four-Criteria Model.



Working from the Bottom Up: While priorities logically flow top-down, GTD suggests starting “bottom-up” (Ground and Horizon 1). Gaining control of the current, mundane details clears the inner decks, allowing the mind to focus constructively on the more meaningful and elusive higher levels without resistance.

Recap

Action choices are governed by the Four-Criteria Model: Context, Time, Energy, and Priority.

Organizing lists by Context is crucial because it ensures that only runnable actions are visible when the resources are available.

Daily work consists of pre-defined work, emerging work (surprises), and defining new work.

The Six-Level Model (Horizons of Focus) provides the hierarchical framework for strategic prioritization.

Horizons 2 (Areas of Focus) helps generate projects to maintain standards and roles.

Operating from the bottom up (Ground and Horizon 1) provides the foundation of control and trust necessary before focusing effectively on higher goals (Horizons 3–5).




18.3.7 Getting Projects Under Control
This chapter addresses the methods for deep, or vertical, thinking on projects that require more planning beyond simply defining the next action.

18.3.7.1 Planning Steps for Projects
When a project is stuck or requires more clarity, planning actions must be defined and executed.

Brainstorming Action: If the Next Action is “Draft ideas re X,” this action is placed on the @Computer or @Anywhere list, depending on the preferred medium (digital vs. paper mind map).

Organizing Action: If notes are collected, the next action may be “Organize Project X notes,” placed on the @Office or @Anywhere list based on location of files.

Information Gathering: The next action may be to gather information needed before planning can begin (e.g., “Surf Web for vendor options”).



18.3.7.2 Tools and Structures to Support Deep Thinking
Informal, external tools are key to sustaining creative thought beyond the mind’s limited focus time (a few minutes).

Writing Tools: High-quality pens and perforated paper should be universally accessible to capture ideas quickly.

Whiteboards/Easels: Provide large visual space for brainstorming, helping anchor thinking and fostering ad hoc visual communication.

Software Tools: Mind-mapping and outlining applications are highly useful for capturing and structuring informal project plans digitally.

Project Files: Separate file folders or loose-leaf pages should be created for new topics immediately, even if only containing raw notes.







Practical Application: Using Ideas Effectively




Clear the Deck: Before a deep-thinking session, clear the environment and minimize distractions.


Focused Engagement: Use the Natural Planning Model (NPM) steps as a structure for the session: start with Purpose, move to Vision, then capture all related ideas (Brainstorm).


Translate Thinking: Translate the resulting plan into the first Next Action and ensure that thought fragments are filed as Project Support Material.




Recap

Most projects require minimal planning, but the most complex 5% may need a deliberate application of the Natural Planning Model.

More informal, back-of-the-envelope planning is required more often than formal project management.

The next action for an ambiguous project is often “Draft ideas re X” or “Organize Project X notes”.

External tools like whiteboards, pens, and paper pads help anchor thinking and are essential for focused engagement over long periods.

The goal of vertical planning is to move a project from the stuck state to a clear next action, enabling horizontal movement.

title: 18  Getting Things Done
section: 18.4 The Power of the Key Principles
text:
18.4 The Power of the Key Principles

18.4.1 The Power of the Capturing Habit
The discipline of capturing 100% of “stuff” creates profound personal and organizational benefits.

18.4.1.1 Personal Benefits of Capturing
The initial experience of a full capture is often described as feeling “so bad, and yet so good”. The bad part is acknowledging the full, overwhelming inventory; the good part is the immediate relief and clarity from externalizing it.

Integrity and Trust: When agreements with oneself are externalized, they can be renegotiated consciously. It is impossible to renegotiate agreements you cannot remember making, yet the subconscious holds you accountable for them, creating stress.

Saying No with Integrity: Maintaining a complete inventory makes it easier to decline new commitments because one has an objective view of the current workload.

Eliminating Psychological Drag: Items held in the head take up disproportionately more or less attention than they deserve. Capturing everything eliminates this “dull sameness” of pressure and frees the mind to focus on the task at hand.

Radical Departure: Traditional time management errs by suggesting that unimportant tasks are not worth tracking. In GTD, everything is tracked—because if it is on the mind, it is consuming energy that must be released.



18.4.1.2 Organizational Benefits
When a culture adopts the 100% capture standard, communications tighten, and trust increases.

Reducing “Interrupt-itis”: Organizations with poor capture systems suffer from constant interruptions because people cannot trust putting communications into the system. Timely responses via the trusted system (e.g., emptying an e-mail inbox) reduce the perceived need for face-to-face follow-up meetings.

Facilitating Renegotiation: A complete capture system allows for continuous, healthy renegotiation of commitments, which is critical in a world where everyone has more to do than they can manage.

Tight Ship: Collective adoption of the capture standard creates a foundation of efficiency and focus, even if the strategic direction (Horizons 3–5) needs adjustment.


Recap

Capturing 100% of commitments (open loops) is necessary for the mind to fully relax.

The subconscious part of the mind holds all commitments in the present tense, creating failure and stress if they cannot all be done immediately.

Capturing allows conscious, objective renegotiation of agreements, strengthening integrity.

In a capture-focused culture, trust in the system increases, minimizing unnecessary interruptions and follow-up.

Uncaptured incompletions create a “psychological drag” that limits clarity and focus.




18.4.2 The Power of the Next-Action Decision
The commitment to defining the next action as a standard operating query is transformative, leading to increased energy, clarity, accountability, and productivity.

18.4.2.1 Why Next Action is Critical
The next-action technique (pioneered by Dean Acheson) forces the necessary front-end thinking before a situation turns into a crisis.

Creating the Option of Doing: Most “stuck” projects are stuck because the specific next physical step (e.g., “E-mail Fred for info re: the garage” vs. “Get a tune-up”) has not been defined.

Intelligent Dumbing Down: Deciding the next action intelligently “dumbs down” the overwhelming picture of a complex project (e.g., taxes) into a single, concrete, doable task. This shifts focus from the paralyzing negative image to a positive, winnable step.

Procrastination: Bright, sensitive people procrastinate most because their creative minds easily visualize all the potential negative complexities and failures of a project (e.g., getting audited), causing immediate resistance. Defining the next action circumvents this resistance.



18.4.2.2 Organizational Value of a Next-Action Standard

Clarity: It forces discussions to end with a clear conclusion about whether an action is needed, what it is, and who owns it, rather than a vague sense of decision.

Accountability: By defining who is responsible for the next physical step, accountability is immediately established.

Productivity: It shifts behavior from avoiding thinking until the last minute (when things “blow up”) to proactively defining actions on the front end.

Empowerment: Taking small, defined steps builds momentum and belief in the ability to achieve results.


Recap

“What’s the next action?” should become a standard query to force clarity and execution.

Projects get stuck because the next physical, visible step is undefined.

Defining the next action “intelligently dumbs down” overwhelming problems, replacing negative imagery with positive, achievable tasks.

Sensitive people often procrastinate because they over-visualize the complex difficulties of a task.

The organizational benefits of the next-action standard include clarity, immediate accountability, and increased momentum.




18.4.3 The Power of Outcome Focusing
Focusing on the desired outcome (project definition) ties high-level vision to mundane daily activity and is essential for productivity.

18.4.3.1 Significance of Applied Outcome Thinking
Defining the successful outcome is necessary before defining the next action; they are interconnected.

Identifying Incompletes: Everything experienced as incomplete must have a reference point for “complete”.

Multilevel Outcome Management: The key questions—“What does this mean to me?” and “What do I want to be true about this (desired outcome)?”—must be answered for every item, linking high-level purpose to the smallest detail.

The Fast Track: By applying GTD principles in the trenches, individuals accelerate their professional and personal development because they demonstrate a reliable ability to execute.

Productivity at its Best: Defining specific projects and next actions that address real quality-of-life issues (e.g., spending time with family) is a high manifestation of productivity.



18.4.3.2 The Power of Natural Planning
The Natural Planning Model provides an integrated, flexible framework that connects intention to execution.

Purpose: Healthy and mature to challenge the “why” of any activity.

Vision/Outcome: Being comfortable creating visions of success before the methods are clear is a phenomenal trait.

Brainstorming: Willingness to generate and capture all ideas without judgment accesses creative intelligence.

Organizing: Mental discipline required to hone ideas into components and sequences.

Next Action: Moving on something in the physical world is the essence of productivity.

When outcome focusing becomes an organizational standard (asking “What do we want to accomplish with this?”), it replaces negativity and resistance with focused direction.

Recap

Outcome focusing links high-level vision to the concrete execution required in the short run.

Project definition and next action definition are inseparable; one informs the other.

The ability to execute effectively at the operational level (GTD fundamentals) moves individuals faster toward higher career and life goals.

Defining desired outcomes for subtle concerns (e.g., quality of life, relationship issues) is productivity in its highest form.

The Natural Planning Model is a cohesive model for thinking through situations, ensuring consistency from purpose down to action.




18.4.4 GTD and Cognitive Science
Since the first edition, scientific research has validated the efficacy of GTD principles, providing a foundation for why the methods produce greater clarity, control, and focus.







Scientific Validation Category
GTD Correlation




Distributed Cognition / External Mind
Research confirms the mind is poor at holding (remembering) ideas but excellent at having (pattern detection) them. GTD provides an external brain (the trusted system) to store triggers, freeing the brain for creative thinking.


Relieving Cognitive Load of Incompletions
Unfinished goals consume mental capacity (cognitive load). Research confirms that completion is not required to relieve the burden—only a trusted plan that ensures forward engagement is needed. Defining the next action is a sufficient “plan” if the reminder is parked in a trusted place.


Flow Theory
GTD practices, such as clearing the mind and focusing attention on one task, enable individuals to find the state of “flow” (optimal experience) more easily. Completing tasks and tracking progress (feedback) also support flow.


Self-Leadership Theory
GTD supports all three categories of self-leadership strategies (behavior-focused, natural reward, constructive thought patterns). The system provides self-cuing (physical artifacts spurring future action) and helps shift the mind-set from overwhelmed to motivated (constructive thought).


Implementation Intentions
This research shows that connecting an action to a specific context (a cause-and-effect link) ensures the behavior is automatically enacted. GTD lists (e.g., “When I am @Home, I will [Action]”) function as optimal implementation intentions.


Psychological Capital (PsyCap)
GTD builds the four components of high PsyCap (Self-efficacy, Optimism, Resilience, and Hope). Systematically defining next actions and achieving small wins builds confidence and control (Self-efficacy). The ability to recalibrate the system quickly during stress builds Resilience.




Recap

Cognitive science provides a formal validation for the effectiveness of GTD principles.

GTD functions as an “external brain” (distributed cognition), freeing the mind from memory storage.

The cognitive burden of unfinished tasks is relieved by simply defining the next action and placing the reminder in a trusted system.

GTD practices enhance the ability to achieve the psychological state of “flow” by promoting clear goals and singular focus.
